In 1890, Cassatt was captivated by a large exhibition of Japanese prints in Paris. She then created a series of color prints influenced by Japanese aesthetics. She continued this investigation across different media. The result is this bold composition. It features a dramatically flattened picture plane, decorative patterning, and a bright palette.

CuratorialText: René Magritte painted Time Transfixed at a moment when he was attempting to elicit “poetic secrets” through his works. With both philosophy and psychology in mind, the artist challenged himself to juxtapose two images in such a way that they would suggest in the viewer’s mind a third un-pictured thing. Here he connected two highly disparate objects—a train and a fireplace—in a composition that became one of his most iconic works. Although the fireplace is domestic and the train is industrial, they are linked by the smoke. Magritte understood that viewers would connect the locomotive’s billowing exhaust with the plumes of smoke that travel up chimneys, uncovering the painting’s poetic secret.
persona2_adapted: René Magritte painted *Time Transfixed* at a moment when he was trying to reveal “poetic secrets” through his works. With both philosophy and psychology in mind, the artist challenged himself to place two images next to each other. He wanted them to suggest, in the viewer’s mind, a third thing that is not actually painted. In this work, he connected two very different objects—a train and a fireplace—in a composition that became one of his most iconic works. Even though the fireplace is domestic and the train is industrial, they are linked by the smoke. Magritte understood that viewers would connect the locomotive’s billowing exhaust with the plumes of smoke that rise up chimneys. In doing so, they would discover the painting’s poetic secret.

CuratorialText: One of the most avant-garde aspects of the Impressionists was their choice of subject matter, which frequently included scenes derived from modern, industrial Paris, from iron bridges to exhibition halls to train sheds. The train station at Saint-Lazare would have been a familiar, meaningful sight to Claude Monet in the 1870s. The terminal linked Paris to Normandy, where the artist developed his technique of painting outdoors in the 1860s. It was also the point of departure for the towns and villages west and north of Paris that the Impressionists frequently visited. Monet completed eight of his twelve known paintings of the Gare Saint-Lazare in time for the third Impressionist exhibition, in 1877, probably displaying them in the same gallery.
persona2_adapted: One of the most avant-garde aspects of the Impressionists was their choice of subject matter. They often depicted scenes from modern, industrial Paris. These included iron bridges, exhibition halls, and train sheds. In the 1870s, the Gare Saint-Lazare train station would have been a familiar and meaningful sight for Claude Monet. The terminal connected Paris to Normandy. It was in Normandy that Monet developed his technique of painting outdoors in the 1860s. The station was also the departure point for towns and villages west and north of Paris. The Impressionists frequently visited these places. Monet completed eight of his twelve known paintings of the Gare Saint-Lazare in time for the third Impressionist exhibition in 1877. He probably displayed them in the same gallery.

CuratorialText: Monet chose to focus his attention here on the glass-and-iron train shed, where he found an appealing combination of artiﬁ cial and natural effects—the rising steam of locomotives trapped within the structure and the daylight penetrating large, glazed sections of the roof, for instance. Monet’s depictions of the station inaugurated what was to become for him an established pattern of painting a speciﬁc motif repeatedly in order to capture subtle and temporal atmospheric changes, as in his famous series of stacks of wheat. But the Saint-Lazare paintings also represented his last attempt to capture urban life: from this point on in his career, Monet largely devoted himself to landscapes.
persona2_adapted: Monet chose to focus here on the glass-and-iron train shed. He found an appealing combination of artificial and natural effects in this space. For example, the rising steam from locomotives was trapped inside the structure, while daylight penetrated the large, glazed sections of the roof. Monet’s depictions of the station began what became an established pattern for him: painting a specific motif repeatedly to capture subtle, temporary atmospheric changes. This is similar to his famous series of stacks of wheat. However, the Saint-Lazare paintings also represent his last attempt to capture urban life. From this point onward in his career, Monet largely devoted himself to landscapes.

CuratorialText: Salvador Dalí, Surrealism’s most publicized practitioner, created monstrous visions of a world turned inside out, which he made even more compelling through his extraordinary technical skills. When the Art Institute acquired Inventions of the Monsters in 1943, the artist wrote his congratulations and explained.
persona2_adapted: Salvador Dalí, the most widely publicized practitioner of Surrealism, created monstrous visions of a world turned inside out. He made these visions even more compelling through his extraordinary technical skills. When the Art Institute acquired *Inventions of the Monsters* in 1943, the artist wrote to congratulate the museum and provided an explanation.

CuratorialText: “According to Nostradamus the apparition of monsters presages the outbreak of war. The canvas was painted in the Semmering mountains near Vienna a few months before the Anschluss [the 1938 political union of Austria and Germany] and has a prophetic character. Horse women equal maternal river monsters. Flaming giraffe equals masculine apocalyptic monster. Cat angel equals divine heterosexual monster. Hourglass equals metaphysical monster. Gala and Dalí equal sentimental monster. The little blue dog is not a true monster.”
persona2_adapted: "According to Nostradamus, the appearance of monsters foreshadows the outbreak of war. This painting was created in the Semmering mountains near Vienna, a few months before the Anschluss [the 1938 political union of Austria and Germany]. It has a prophetic character. Horse women correspond to maternal river monsters. A flaming giraffe corresponds to a masculine apocalyptic monster. A cat angel corresponds to a divine heterosexual monster. An hourglass corresponds to a metaphysical monster. Gala and Dalí correspond to a sentimental monster. The little blue dog is not a true monster."

CuratorialText: Inventions of the Monsters has an ominous mood. It is rife with threats of danger, from the menacing fire in the distance to the sibylline figure in the foreground with an hourglass and a butterfly, both symbols of the inevitability of death. Next to this figure sit Dalí and his wife and muse, Gala. With his native Catalonia embroiled in the Spanish Civil War, the artist surely felt great anxiety over a world without a safe haven, a world that indeed had allowed for the invention of monsters.
persona2_adapted: Inventions of the Monsters has an ominous mood. The painting is full of threats of danger. These threats range from the menacing fire in the distance to the sibylline figure in the foreground. This figure holds an hourglass and a butterfly. Both are symbols of the inevitability of death. Next to this figure sit Dalí and his wife and muse, Gala. At that time, Dalí’s native Catalonia was involved in the Spanish Civil War. The artist surely felt great anxiety. He worried about a world without a safe place. He saw a world that had indeed allowed for the invention of monsters.

CuratorialText: Shiva, one of the most important Hindu divinities, is here depicted as the Lord of the Dance (Nataraja), an iconic image in Indian art. Shiva’s cosmic dance sets in motion the rhythm of life and death; it pervades the universe, as symbolized by the ring of fire that is filled with the loose, snakelike locks of the god’s hair. One pair of his arms balances the flame of destruction and the hand drum (damaru) that beats the rhythm of life while another performs symbolic gestures: the raised right hand means “fear not,” and the left hand (gajahasta) pointing down toward his raised left foot signifies release from the ignorance that hinders realization of the ultimate reality. Shiva is shown perfectly balanced, with his right leg planted on the demon of darkness (Apasmara), stamping out ignorance. The tiny figure of the personified river goddess, Ganga, is caught up in his matted, flowing locks. Shiva was believed to break the fall of the great Ganges River as it descends from the Himalayas by standing beneath the waters, which divide over his hair, becoming the seven holy rivers of India. This classic bronze comes from the Chola period in the south of India. Icons such as this were carried in procession during religious ceremonies.

Shiva’s cosmic dance sets in motion the rhythm of life and death. This dance fills the universe, as symbolized by the ring of fire, which is filled with the loose, snakelike locks of the god’s hair. One pair of his arms holds the flame of destruction and the hand drum (damaru), which beats the rhythm of life. Another pair performs symbolic gestures: the raised right hand means “fear not,” and the left hand (gajahasta), pointing down toward his raised left foot, signifies release from the ignorance that blocks realization of the ultimate reality. Shiva is shown perfectly balanced. His right leg is planted on the demon of darkness (Apasmara), stamping out ignorance. The tiny figure of the personified river goddess, Ganga, is caught in his matted, flowing locks. According to belief, Shiva broke the fall of the great Ganges River as it descended from the Himalayas. He stood beneath the waters, which divided over his hair and became the seven holy rivers of India. This classic bronze comes from the Chola period in the south of India. Icons such as this were carried in procession during religious ceremonies.

CuratorialText: Albert Bierstadt is best remembered for his paintings of the American West, but during his early career, the ambitious artist also created New England landscapes, particularly of the White Mountains, as seen here. When this painting was first exhibited in 1863, critics declared it Bierstadt’s “best work” and praised the artist’s deft contrast of “light and shade,” which brought a heightened realism to the image. Bierstadt’s affinity for the White Mountains mirrored a growing interest in the region as one of America’s premier tourist attractions. Photographs of the area from the 19th century suggest that Bierstadt utilized landscape elements from the Flume, a popular New Hampshire tourist site, in this imaginary composition.
persona2_adapted:
Albert Bierstadt is best known for his paintings of the American West. However, early in his career, the ambitious artist also created landscapes of New England, especially the White Mountains, as seen in this work.

When this painting was first exhibited in 1863, critics called it Bierstadt’s “best work.” They praised his skillful contrast of “light and shade,” which gave the image a heightened sense of realism.

Bierstadt’s strong connection to the White Mountains reflected a growing interest in the region as one of America’s main tourist destinations. Nineteenth-century photographs of the area suggest that Bierstadt used landscape elements from the Flume—a popular tourist site in New Hampshire—in this imaginary composition.

CuratorialText: In this large-scale canvas, one of several Paul Gauguin sent back to France in 1897, women of varying ages appear to go about daily life within a colorful, stylized outdoor environment. Like much of the artist’s work, this painting combines observed detail with artistic fantasy to create a dreamlike scene whose narrative resists simple interpretation. The work’s deliberately provocative title enhances this effect, leaving us to wonder if the “you” refers to someone in the painting or to the viewer.
persona2_adapted: In this large-scale canvas—one of several Paul Gauguin sent back to France in 1897—women of different ages appear to go about their daily lives in a colorful, stylized outdoor setting. As with much of the artist’s work, this painting combines observed details with artistic fantasy to create a dreamlike scene. Its narrative resists easy interpretation. The deliberately provocative title reinforces this effect. It leaves us to wonder whether the “you” refers to someone inside the painting or to the viewer.

CuratorialText: Motivations for travel throughout history have been almost as varied as the means of travel. Whether people traveled alone or in groups, on foot, on animals, in caravans, on ships, or using modern modes of transportation like planes, trains, and automobiles, art can capture how and why we move through the world.
persona2_adapted: Throughout history, the reasons for travel have been nearly as diverse as the methods of travel. Whether people journeyed alone or in groups, on foot, on animals, in caravans, on ships, or using modern forms of transportation such as planes, trains, and automobiles, art can capture both how and why we move through the world.

CuratorialText: Marco Polo (1254–1324) was a Venetian merchant who spent about seventeen years in China—from around 1274 to 1292—during the reign of the Mongol emperor Khubilai Khan (reigned 1260–94), founder of the Yuan dynasty. In 1276, Khubilai’s armies conquered the Chinese capital at Hangzhou with minimal destruction. Sometime after this, Marco Polo visited the city and its West Lake.
persona2_adapted: Marco Polo (1254–1324) was a Venetian merchant. He spent approximately seventeen years in China, from around 1274 to 1292. This period coincided with the reign of the Mongol emperor Khubilai Khan (reigned 1260–94), who founded the Yuan dynasty. In 1276, Khubilai’s armies conquered the Chinese capital at Hangzhou. The conquest caused minimal destruction. Sometime after this event, Marco Polo visited the city and its West Lake.

CuratorialText: Travel is a common theme in traditional Chinese landscape painting. From ancient times, the emperors of China maintained a network of roads and waterways that unified their vast realm and facilitated the movement of people and goods. Owing to this imperial association, images of ordinary travelers on the empire’s roads and rivers connoted the benevolent rule of an enlightened sovereign and evoked an era of peace and prosperity. Rather than documenting the hardships or actual circumstances of any particular journey, images of anonymous characters traveling or working together capture some of the noteworthy sights and scenes a traveler might encounter along the way. Simultaneously, they promote the fundamental ideals of a harmonious society and a well-ordered state.
persona2_adapted: Travel is a common theme in traditional Chinese landscape painting. Since ancient times, China’s emperors maintained a network of roads and waterways. This system unified their vast territory and made it easier for people and goods to move. Because of this connection to imperial power, images of ordinary travelers on the empire’s roads and rivers suggested the wise rule of an enlightened ruler. They also brought to mind a time of peace and prosperity. These images do not document the difficulties or real conditions of any specific journey. Instead, they show anonymous figures traveling or working together. These scenes capture notable sights and moments a traveler might see along the way. At the same time, they promote the core ideals of a harmonious society and a well-ordered state.

CuratorialText: One of the most enduring motivations for travel is religious devotion. Nearly every major religion—Christianity, Judaism, Islam, Buddhism, Hinduism, Jainism, and Sikhism, among others—has a tradition of pilgrimage: intentional travel to a site of spiritual significance. Christian pilgrims traveled to the Holy Land to walk in the footsteps of Christ or visited more local destinations connected to the lives or relics of the saints. In Islam, pilgrimage (hajj) to Mecca is one of the principal duties of all believers. Other holy sites were also visited on a regular basis. Over many centuries, hundreds of Chinese, Japanese, and Korean pilgrims braved countless dangers to visit places where the Historical Buddha lived and taught. Pilgrims would sometimes bring gifts, called votive offerings, with them as a donation to the destination site.
persona2_adapted: One of the most enduring motivations for travel is religious devotion. Nearly every major religion—Christianity, Judaism, Islam, Buddhism, Hinduism, Jainism, and Sikhism, among others—has a tradition of pilgrimage: intentional travel to a site of spiritual significance. Christian pilgrims traveled to the Holy Land to walk in the footsteps of Christ, or visited more local destinations connected to the lives or relics of the saints. In Islam, pilgrimage (hajj) to Mecca is one of the principal duties of all believers. Other holy sites were also visited on a regular basis. Over many centuries, hundreds of Chinese, Japanese, and Korean pilgrims braved countless dangers to visit places where the Historical Buddha lived and taught. Pilgrims would sometimes bring gifts, called votive offerings, with them as a donation to the destination site.

CuratorialText: Trade was another common motivation for travel and occurred in many forms, but the most famous trade route in history was the Silk Road, the overland trade routes that linked the prosperous Tang empire (618–907) with Central, West, and South Asia. On the Silk Road, foreign merchants joined Buddhist missionaries, diplomatic envoys, translators, craftsmen, entertainers, and other skilled immigrants in a wealthy, worldly environment that offered a ready market for exotic imports, including silver and gold objects, delicate glassware, and even grape wine.
persona2_adapted: Trade was another common reason for travel and took many forms. However, the most famous trade route in history was the Silk Road. This was a network of overland routes that connected the prosperous Tang empire (618–907) with Central, West, and South Asia. On the Silk Road, foreign merchants traveled together with Buddhist missionaries, diplomatic envoys, translators, craftsmen, entertainers, and other skilled immigrants. They all moved within a wealthy, cosmopolitan environment. This environment offered a ready market for exotic imports. These imports included silver and gold objects, delicate glassware, and even grape wine.

CuratorialText: In the twentieth century, travel offered a key means for diplomacy and cultural exchange. Alice Roosevelt Longworth (1884–1980), Theodore Roosevelt’s daughter, embarked on a tour of Asia accompanying William Howard Taft’s goodwill mission in 1905. This voyage is immortalized in a series of photographs but also through diplomatic gifts that dignitaries, such as the emperor of Japan, bestowed on Roosevelt.
persona2_adapted: In the twentieth century, travel was an important tool for diplomacy and cultural exchange. Alice Roosevelt Longworth (1884–1980), the daughter of President Theodore Roosevelt, joined a goodwill mission led by William Howard Taft in 1905. During this trip, she traveled through Asia. The journey is recorded not only in a series of photographs but also through diplomatic gifts. These gifts were given to Roosevelt by dignitaries, including the emperor of Japan.

CuratorialText: Adventurers, archaeologists, and scholars have long been traversing the globe to conduct research and to explore foreign lands. In the early twentieth century, leisure travel among Europeans and North Americans also became increasingly common. Fascinated by the cultural heritage of Asia and the Middle East, tourists began to expand their horizons by venturing there.
persona2_adapted: Adventurers, archaeologists, and scholars have long traveled across the world to conduct research and explore foreign lands. In the early twentieth century, leisure travel also became more common among Europeans and North Americans. Fascinated by the cultural heritage of Asia and the Middle East, tourists began to broaden their horizons by traveling there.

CuratorialText: Artists who traveled played a pivotal role in documenting and popularizing their visions of the distant places they visited. Their interactions with other artists and the things they experienced on their journeys contributed to the dispersal of different artistic styles and motifs.
persona2_adapted: Artists who traveled played a central role in documenting and popularizing their visions of the distant places they visited. Their interactions with other artists, as well as the things they experienced during their journeys, helped spread different artistic styles and motifs.

CuratorialText: While travel has mostly positive connotations in popular culture today, it’s important to remember the darker sides of travel as well. Not all travel is undertaken by choice: people can be forced to travel as a result of political exile, as well as due to war, famine, disease, economic hardship, environmental disaster, and colonialism. Even when travel is voluntary, it can be dangerous, and this was particularly true in ages past. The physical act of traveling exposed people to potential injuries and diseases, the natural elements were unpredictable and threatened modes of transportation, and there was an inherent unpredictability to being on the road far from home. The recognition of these dangers shaped stories and cultural beliefs.
persona2_adapted: While travel is often associated with positive ideas in popular culture today, it is also important to consider its darker aspects. Not all travel is voluntary: people may be forced to move due to political exile, war, famine, disease, economic hardship, environmental disaster, or colonialism. Even when travel is chosen freely, it can be dangerous—especially in earlier historical periods. The physical act of traveling exposed people to potential injuries and illnesses. Natural conditions were unpredictable and could threaten modes of transport. There was also an inherent unpredictability in being far from home. The awareness of these dangers shaped stories and cultural beliefs.

CuratorialText: Objects themselves have rich histories and often undertook journeys of their own, whether through trade, gift giving, or collecting. For conservators, the journey of an item is embedded within its material presence; marks, scars, repairs, and other interventions describe the actions of others who have contributed to its present state. The process of conservation involves the synthesis of material evidence and its placement in time, not just to understand an item’s past but to envision its future preservation. Anthropologists have described the trajectories of material items in terms of their lives, biographies, or itineraries, but such notions are intuitively familiar to conservators, whose actions are fundamentally additive to an item’s journey, both materially and through time.
persona2_adapted: Objects themselves have rich histories and often undertook journeys of their own, whether through trade, gift giving, or collecting. For conservators, the journey of an item is embedded within its material presence; marks, scars, repairs, and other interventions describe the actions of others who have contributed to its present state. The process of conservation involves the synthesis of material evidence and its placement in time, not just to understand an item’s past but to envision its future preservation. Anthropologists have described the trajectories of material items in terms of their lives, biographies, or itineraries, but such notions are intuitively familiar to conservators, whose actions are fundamentally additive to an item’s journey, both materially and through time.

CuratorialText: Individual travelers carried things with them that were essential for the journey itself. Clothing and equipment suitable to traversing long distances sometimes set them apart from local residents, and travelers carried personal effects and accessories, such as small books, drinking flasks, and protective amulets, to make the journey easier.
persona2_adapted: Individual travelers brought items necessary for the journey itself. Clothing and equipment suited to covering long distances sometimes distinguished them from local inhabitants. Travelers also carried personal belongings and accessories—such as small books, drinking flasks, and protective amulets—to make the journey easier.

CuratorialText: Travelers brought objects acquired on their journeys home with them as well. Holy objects acquired by pilgrims allowed them to bring the blessings of their pilgrimage back from their destination. Images made during travel—whether sketches, paintings, or photographs—captured the experience of the journey for future reflection. Souvenirs purchased during a trip also offered travelers a way of commemorating a journey and gave them something tangible on which to reflect. The advent of commercial global travel networks in many cultures and contexts was accompanied by a flood of photographs, drawings, postcards, mementos, and other means of recording scientific and sentimental experiences abroad. Perhaps the most recognizable artifact of travel over the past 150 years is the picture postcard. These were collected as mementos or mailed to family and friends from abroad, accruing the additional prestige of a foreign stamp and postmark.
persona2_adapted:
Travelers also brought home objects they had acquired during their journeys. Holy objects obtained by pilgrims allowed them to carry the blessings of their pilgrimage back from the destination. Images made during travel—whether sketches, paintings, or photographs—captured the experience of the journey for later reflection. Souvenirs purchased during a trip gave travelers a way to commemorate the journey and provided them with something tangible to reflect upon.

The emergence of commercial global travel networks in many cultures and contexts was accompanied by a flood of photographs, drawings, postcards, mementos, and other means of recording scientific and sentimental experiences abroad. Perhaps the most recognizable artifact of travel over the past 150 years is the picture postcard. These were collected as mementos or mailed to family and friends from abroad, gaining the additional prestige of a foreign stamp and postmark.

CuratorialText: Most travel we think of involves physically moving between places, but virtual travel (colloquially, “armchair travel”) that takes place in the mind spans time and cultures and is facilitated by books and works of art. Through focused contemplation of a travel account, a map, a cherished souvenir, or images of far-off places, memory and imagination combine to transport someone mentally and to simulate the experience of travel.
persona2_adapted: Most travel involves physically moving from one place to another. However, virtual travel—often called "armchair travel"—takes place in the mind. It spans different times and cultures. Books and works of art make this kind of travel possible. When someone focuses deeply on a travel account, a map, a treasured souvenir, or images of distant places, memory and imagination work together. This combination can mentally transport the person and create a simulated experience of travel.

CuratorialText: While it may not be the first thing we think of when looking at a work of art, environmental factors have shaped everything about it, from how it was made, to how it has been perceived and appreciated, to how it has been preserved for today’s audiences. Dive into the complex and interconnected relationships between art and the environment—from images of the natural world to recycling ancient jades—to see our collections in a whole new way.
persona2_adapted: Although it may not be the first thing we consider when looking at a work of art, environmental factors have shaped nearly every aspect of it. These factors influence how the work was made, how it has been seen and valued, and how it has been preserved for contemporary audiences. Explore the complex and interconnected relationships between art and the environment—from depictions of the natural world to the reuse of ancient jades—and discover a new perspective on our collections.

CuratorialText: While environmental conditions differ from place to place, contact with nature has been a universal human experience. It’s therefore not surprising that natural motifs—plants, animals, water, the sky—adorn works of art across all cultures and are a consistent source of inspiration and meaning.
persona2_adapted: While environmental conditions vary from one place to another, contact with nature is a universal human experience. It is therefore not surprising that natural motifs—such as plants, animals, water, and the sky—appear in works of art across all cultures. These motifs are a consistent source of inspiration and meaning.

CuratorialText: Depictions of landscapes can serve as valuable documentation of specific places, but they can also evoke strong emotions. Images of the natural world can remind us of the beauty and power of nature, evoking feelings of tranquility, wonder, awe, and contemplation.
persona2_adapted: Depictions of landscapes can function as important records of specific places. At the same time, they can also provoke intense emotions. Images of the natural world can remind us of nature’s beauty and force. They may bring about feelings of calm, wonder, awe, and deep thought.

CuratorialText: From the earliest Japanese poetry anthology, compiled in the eighth century, to the present day, the beauty of the Japanese landscape has been celebrated in literature and art. At first, landscapes were not an independent subject but were incorporated into the backgrounds of Buddhist paintings, seasonal subjects (shikinami-e), and paintings of famous scenic or sacred sites (meisho-e). Beginning around the thirteenth century, possibly through the influence of the introduction of new modes of ink painting from China and Korea, landscape became an independent subject in Japanese painting. Landscapes of Japan were most often, but not exclusively, painted in the full color mode, which effectively expressed the seasonal changes that are inextricably linked with natural imagery in Japanese poetry and art. Ink painting was usually chosen for varied and dramatic landscapes of China, which most Japanese artists understood through their study of imported paintings, and later, of painting manuals as well. Whether rendered in rich, deep colors or in subtle tones of ink, landscapes by Japanese artists are imbued with a sense of intimate understanding and appreciation of the transient beauty and spiritual significance of the natural world.

At first, landscapes were not an independent subject. Instead, they appeared in the backgrounds of Buddhist paintings, seasonal subjects (shikinami-e), and paintings of famous scenic or sacred sites (meisho-e). Around the thirteenth century, landscape became an independent subject in Japanese painting. This may have been influenced by the introduction of new ink painting techniques from China and Korea. Landscapes of Japan were most often, but not exclusively, painted in full color mode. This mode effectively expressed the seasonal changes that are deeply connected to natural imagery in Japanese poetry and art. Ink painting was usually chosen for varied and dramatic landscapes of China. Most Japanese artists understood these landscapes through their study of imported paintings, and later, through painting manuals as well. Whether rendered in rich, deep colors or in subtle tones of ink, landscapes by Japanese artists show a sense of intimate understanding and appreciation. They capture the transient beauty and spiritual significance of the natural world.

CuratorialText: Travel to misty mountain valleys and foggy ocean beaches through the eyes of great Japanese artists from the sixteenth century on, accompanied by the sounds of the shakuhachi (bamboo flute), in this exploration of nature as an inspiration for Japanese art and music, guided by the virtuoso Kurahashi Yodo II and curator Frank Feltens.
persona2_adapted: Travel through misty mountain valleys and foggy ocean beaches as seen by great Japanese artists from the sixteenth century onward. You will be accompanied by the sounds of the shakuhachi (bamboo flute). This exploration looks at nature as a source of inspiration for Japanese art and music. It is guided by the virtuoso Kurahashi Yodo II and curator Frank Feltens.

CuratorialText: For Chinese scholar-officials, viewing landscape paintings not only provided aesthetic delight, but a landscape setting was often vested with meaning, which could range from providing a clue about the season of the painting to conveying more complex meanings. Seeing an image of figures in a remote landscape often referred to the key cultural and political concept of eremitism. The literati, or scholar class, believed in the Confucian notion that government service was a high moral duty. However, if the emperor was corrupt, or if a scholar was faced with serving a dynasty founded by usurping foreign rulers then, following the tradition of eremitism, a true gentleman refused government office. Instead, he became a mountain recluse, or at least assumed the demeanor of a hermit or rustic fisherman, to protest the corruption of moral order. Vested with meaning as a symbol of ethical reclusion, landscape motifs had poignant significance.
persona2_adapted:
For Chinese scholar-officials, viewing landscape paintings was not only a source of aesthetic pleasure. A landscape setting was often vested with meaning. This meaning could range from a clue about the season of the painting to more complex interpretations. For example, an image of figures in a remote landscape often referred to the key cultural and political concept of eremitism.

The literati, or scholar class, believed in the Confucian idea that government service was a high moral duty. However, if the emperor was corrupt, or if a scholar had to serve a dynasty founded by usurping foreign rulers, then—following the tradition of eremitism—a true gentleman refused government office. Instead, he became a mountain recluse, or at least adopted the demeanor of a hermit or rustic fisherman. This was a way to protest the corruption of moral order.

Vested with meaning as a symbol of ethical reclusion, landscape motifs carried poignant significance.

CuratorialText: Careful observation of nature did not only translate to depictions of real places. Artists used their knowledge of the natural world to create imagined landscapes as well.
persona2_adapted: Careful observation of nature did not only result in depictions of real places. Artists also used their knowledge of the natural world to create imagined landscapes.

CuratorialText: A full appreciation of Chinese art includes embracing the category of “scholar’s rocks,” alternatively called “spirit stones.”  Scholar’s rocks consist of wondrously shaped and textured rocks, often of limestone, that are collected for placement in a designed garden or are brought indoors for display in a scholar’s study. The size can range from twelve feet or more to tiny enough to be enclosed in a fist. Philosophic and religious texts as well as poetry have a long history of singling out mountains and rocks for special consideration. A saying from the time of Confucius (551 BCE–479 BCE) notes, “The wise take joy in water; the humane take joy in mountains.” At the same time, Daoists pictured mountains as sanctuaries or paradises for immortal beings and used minerals in elixirs for infinite life. Placement of evocative rocks in gardens and homes helped turn a mundane space into a paradise, or at least into an environment conducive to cultivating personal longevity or to the pursuit of eternal life.
persona2_adapted:
To fully appreciate Chinese art, one must understand the category of "scholar’s rocks," also called "spirit stones." These are rocks—often limestone—with unusual shapes and textures. They are collected for display in designed gardens or indoors, in a scholar’s study. Their size can range from over twelve feet to small enough to fit in a fist.

Philosophical and religious texts, as well as poetry, have long given special attention to mountains and rocks. A saying from the time of Confucius (551 BCE–479 BCE) states: “The wise take joy in water; the humane take joy in mountains.” At the same time, Daoists viewed mountains as sanctuaries or paradises for immortal beings. They also used minerals in elixirs to seek infinite life.

Placing evocative rocks in gardens and homes helped turn an ordinary space into a paradise—or at least into an environment that supports personal longevity or the pursuit of eternal life.

CuratorialText: In Chinese tradition, the educated class delighted in rocks as tangible embodiments of the powers of creation and cosmic transformation, and they sometimes rhapsodized about them as eternally faithful “friends.” Gazing upon them could encourage imaginary travel deep into nature. Chinese scholars, poets, painters, and emperors all engaged in collecting rocks, and painters often studied the rocks they collected, treating them as both muse and model for their landscape paintings.
persona2_adapted: In Chinese tradition, the educated class took pleasure in rocks as physical embodiments of the forces of creation and cosmic change. They sometimes spoke of them with great enthusiasm as eternally loyal “friends.” Looking at these rocks could inspire imaginary journeys deep into nature. Chinese scholars, poets, painters, and emperors all collected rocks. Painters often studied the rocks they had collected, treating them as both a source of inspiration and a model for their landscape paintings.

CuratorialText: From intimate courtyards to monumental temple, tomb, and pleasure gardens, Asia has been central to the development of cultivated landscapes. The earliest known garden—the biblical Garden of Eden—may have been located in West Asia at the confluence of the Tigris and the Euphrates in present-day Iraq. The very word “paradise” is derived from the walled orchard gardens and hunting parks of ancient Iran, referred to as pardis. This type of enclosed garden became an integral part of Persian architectural landscape for centuries and also often included pavilions to provide shade and relief from the sun. According to written sources, the earliest gardens in China, dating to the Zhou period (ca. 1050–256 BCE), consisted of enclosed hunting grounds reserved for the royal elite. Chinese gardens were often characterized by carefully positioned rocks and pools intended to recreate microcosms of nature at large. In Japan, gardens followed a more naturalistic design and incorporated rolling hills and languid ponds to underline harmony between humans and their surroundings. In the arid landscapes of West and South Asia, one of the most common garden plans depended on a series of interconnected pools and axial watercourses.
persona2_adapted:
From intimate courtyards to monumental temples, tombs, and pleasure gardens, Asia has been central to the development of designed landscapes. The earliest known garden—the Garden of Eden from the Bible—may have been located in West Asia, where the Tigris and Euphrates rivers meet, in present-day Iraq. The word “paradise” comes from the walled orchard gardens and hunting parks of ancient Iran, which were called *pardis*. This type of enclosed garden became a key part of Persian architectural landscape for centuries. These gardens often included pavilions to provide shade and protection from the sun.

According to written sources, the earliest gardens in China date from the Zhou period (around 1050–256 BCE). They were enclosed hunting grounds reserved for the royal elite. Chinese gardens were often defined by carefully placed rocks and pools. These elements were meant to recreate small versions of nature as a whole. In Japan, gardens followed a more naturalistic design. They included rolling hills and calm ponds to emphasize harmony between humans and their environment. In the dry landscapes of West and South Asia, one of the most common garden designs used a series of connected pools and straight water channels.

CuratorialText: Throughout history, art has served as a reflection of and response to environmental change. Sometimes this change is deliberately brought about through human beings adapting to and altering their surrounding environment. Cultural landscapes—including the ways that humans have built on, altered, and imagined natural environments—play a significant role in how we understand the world we inhabit.
persona2_adapted: Throughout history, art has reflected and responded to environmental change. Sometimes this change is deliberately caused by humans adapting to and transforming their surroundings. Cultural landscapes—including how humans have built on, changed, and imagined natural environments—play a key role in how we understand the world we live in.

CuratorialText: Works of art can bear witness to the environmental changes that have taken place throughout recorded history. Depictions of animals from regions where those animals no longer exist, for example, can tell us about migrations or extinctions.
persona2_adapted: Works of art can bear witness to environmental changes that have occurred throughout recorded history. For example, images of animals from regions where those animals no longer exist can inform us about migrations or extinctions.

CuratorialText: Humans have always had an impact on the environments where they live, but the scale of that impact dramatically increased with industrialization. In the United States and Europe, factories and advances in transportation sprung up to create a range of consumer goods and ways to travel with new ease and speed. Largely coal powered, these machines spewed pollution that artists such as Whistler sought to visualize in paint.
persona2_adapted: Humans have always affected the environments they inhabit. However, the scale of that impact increased dramatically with industrialization. In the United States and Europe, factories and advances in transportation emerged rapidly. They produced a range of consumer goods and enabled new ease and speed of travel. These machines were largely powered by coal. They emitted pollution that artists such as Whistler sought to visualize in paint.

CuratorialText: Similarly, the infusion of Western technology into Japan from the beginning of the Meiji era (1868–1912) spurred widespread visual documentation of the new developments. Railroad transportation, telegraphic communication, gas-fueled lighting, steam-powered river and ocean vessels, and new materials and designs for bridges and buildings were crammed into the Japanese landscape over two decades.
persona2_adapted: Similarly, the introduction of Western technology into Japan from the start of the Meiji era (1868–1912) led to extensive visual documentation of these new developments. Over two decades, the Japanese landscape was filled with railroads, telegraph systems, gas lighting, steam-powered ships for rivers and oceans, and new materials and designs for bridges and buildings.

CuratorialText: Contemporary artists are increasingly exploring the impact of climate change on the natural world and human communities, whether their goal is to raise awareness about the urgent need to address climate change, to highlight the social and political issues that are bound up in environmental degradation, or to envision alternative futures and inspire hope and action.
persona2_adapted: Contemporary artists are increasingly examining how climate change affects the natural world and human communities. Their goals vary: some aim to raise awareness about the urgent need to address climate change. Others highlight the social and political issues connected to environmental degradation. Still others imagine alternative futures, seeking to inspire hope and action.

CuratorialText: The creation of art itself impacts and is impacted by the environment. Stones and precious metals need to be mined. Wood needs to be harvested. Clay is extracted from the earth. Plants need to be gathered. Many works of art make use of animal products, such as parchment, feathers, horn, and ivory. Different production techniques require burning fuel sources for melting, firing, and glazing. The gathering of raw materials and their transformation into art objects is not without impact on the environment and has at times come at the expense of the exploitation of natural resources and people.
persona2_adapted: The creation of art both affects and is affected by the environment. Stones and precious metals must be mined. Wood must be harvested. Clay is taken from the earth. Plants must be collected. Many artworks use animal products, such as parchment, feathers, horn, and ivory. Different production techniques require burning fuel for melting, firing, and glazing. The collection of raw materials and their transformation into art objects has consequences for the environment. At times, this has come at the cost of exploiting natural resources and people.

CuratorialText: While recycling may seem like a very recent phenomenon, materials have long been reused in the creation of works of art, whether due to the scarcity of materials or to the value placed on an existing object. For example, since jade was such a prestigious medium and a valuable commodity in ancient China, broken or out-of-fashion articles were not discarded but were instead recycled to make new objects. This appears to be especially true in the late Shang dynasty (ca. 1250–1050 BCE). Archaeologists working at the late Shang capital of Anyang have found that, possibly due to the scarcity of resources at a time of high demand, earlier Stone Age (ca. 5000–1700 BCE) objects were being transformed into decorated jewelry and other items that held more interest and meaning to the Shang people. All three pendants shown here were made by cutting up a special kind of Neolithic square tube called a cong, which had lost its ritual significance by Shang times. It is noteworthy that the recycled cong used here represent different Stone Age cultures dispersed across north and south China.
persona2_adapted:
While recycling may seem like a recent phenomenon, materials have actually been reused in art for a long time. This happened either because materials were scarce or because an existing object was considered valuable.  

For example, in ancient China, jade was a highly prestigious material and a valuable commodity. Broken or out-of-fashion jade objects were not thrown away. Instead, they were recycled to create new items. This practice seems to have been especially common during the late Shang dynasty (ca. 1250–1050 BCE).  

Archaeologists working at Anyang, the late Shang capital, have found that earlier Stone Age objects (ca. 5000–1700 BCE) were transformed into decorated jewelry and other items. These new objects held more meaning and interest for the Shang people. This may have been due to a shortage of resources at a time of high demand.  

All three pendants shown here were made by cutting up a special type of Neolithic square tube called a *cong*. By the Shang period, the *cong* had lost its original ritual significance. It is worth noting that the recycled *cong* used here come from different Stone Age cultures, spread across both northern and southern China.

CuratorialText: Environmental conditions can significantly impact the preservation of works of art. Factors such as temperature, humidity, light, and pollution affect the chemical and physical properties of materials in different ways, causing damage and degradation to objects over time. On a larger scale, major environmental changes such as temperature shifts and more destructive weather patterns can threaten heritage sites around the world. Conservators play a pivotal role in safeguarding the objects in the museum’s collections. Read about conservation and scientific research initiatives at the National Museum of Asian Art. The Smithsonian Institution acknowledges climate change as the existential threat of our time. Learn more about the Smithsonian’s sustainability initiatives.

The Smithsonian Institution considers climate change to be the most serious threat of our time. Learn more about the Smithsonian’s sustainability initiatives.

CuratorialText: A color of vitality, passion, good fortune, and celebration, red has undeniable energy. It catches our eye and stirs our blood, forcing us to take notice. From glittering stones to ruddy earth, from insects to minerals, learn about various ways that people have made and understood red through examples drawn from across the museum’s collections.
persona2_adapted: Red is a color of vitality, passion, good fortune, and celebration. It has undeniable energy. It catches our eye and stirs our blood, forcing us to pay attention. From glittering stones to ruddy earth, from insects to minerals, this text explores various ways that people have created and interpreted red. The examples come from across the museum’s collections.

CuratorialText: Several metal compounds (that include iron, mercury, or lead) form commonly used historic colored pigments. These compounds may be found, mined, or produced. Iron compounds in earth pigments can have different colors. Red iron earths are readily found or can be manufactured and have been discovered in many applications, including pottery and paints.
persona2_adapted: Several metal compounds—including those containing iron, mercury, or lead—are commonly used to create historic colored pigments. These compounds can be found in nature, mined, or artificially produced. Iron compounds found in earth pigments can vary in color. Red iron earths are easily obtained from natural sources or can be manufactured. They have been identified in many applications, such as pottery and paints.

CuratorialText: Similarly, mercury-based pigments are made from grinding the red mineral cinnabar. Synthetic versions have long been produced and are referred to as vermilion. This deep red pigment, though toxic, was used in burial ceremonies, incorporated in lacquers, and adorns paintings and documents.

This deep red pigment, although toxic, was used in burial ceremonies, added to lacquers, and appears in paintings and documents.

CuratorialText: While red lead has a naturally forming mineral equivalent (minimum), it was manufactured far more commonly for use as a pigment. In general, lead compounds were heated under conditions favorable to produce the desired colored material and minimizing the formation of others.
persona2_adapted: While red lead has a naturally occurring mineral counterpart (minimum), it was much more commonly manufactured for use as a pigment. In general, lead compounds were heated under conditions that favored the production of the desired colored material, while limiting the formation of other compounds.

CuratorialText: Red copper oxide or cuprite is found as a degradation product on bronzes and forms by the reaction of the copper from the bronze and the atmosphere.
persona2_adapted: Red copper oxide, also known as cuprite, appears as a degradation product on bronzes. It forms through a reaction between the copper in the bronze and the atmosphere.

CuratorialText: Plants and animals can also be sources of reds used in artworks. Madder root and safflower each may have their color extracted, purified, and prepared for use in objects such as textiles and prints. The conditions in which the dye or lake is prepared can change the final color. Scale insects (such as lac and cochineal) are another source. Such insects were harvested, with the red color extracted and prepared for use as a pigment or dye. When possible, determining the colorants used in artworks can shed light on historical trade routes due to the limited native habitat of these insects.

These insects were harvested, and the red color was extracted and prepared for use as a pigment or dye. When possible, identifying the colorants used in artworks can reveal information about historical trade routes, because these insects live only in specific native habitats.

Red also occurred naturally in the form of precious and semi-precious stones, such as rubies, garnets, spinels, and carnelians. Prized for their color and rarity, they were valuable gifts and were often worn by elites or used to adorn luxury items.

Red also occurred naturally in precious and semi-precious stones, such as rubies, garnets, spinels, and carnelians. These stones were valued for their color and rarity. They were often given as important gifts. Elites frequently wore them, or they were used to decorate luxury objects.

CuratorialText: Rubrication, the practice of writing portions of a text in red ink, helped visually underscore certain information and became particularly common in European manuscripts after the seventh century. Red was sometimes used for the angular Kufic script in in copies of the Qur’an made between the eighth and eleventh centuries CE to indicate vocalization; it stood out next to black- or brown-inked calligraphy. Red was also commonly used in the paste for the inked seals that were added to finished works of Chinese painting or calligraphy; it contrasted against black ink writing and images. The seal might give the name of the artist or his studio, the collector, or other private individuals, and it occasionally expresses a favorite brief saying.

There, it contrasted with black ink writing and images. The seal might give the name of the artist or his studio, the collector, or other private individuals. Occasionally, it also expresses a favorite brief saying.

CuratorialText: Love, passion, importance—red carries many positive associations today, and has throughout history. In ancient Egypt, pharaohs were depicted in red due to its association with royalty and passage through the afterlife. In China, red is auspicious—associated with life-generating energy (the sun, blood, and fire)—and is the color of celebrations and prosperity.

In China, red is considered auspicious—it is linked to life-giving energy (the sun, blood, and fire)—and is the color of celebrations and prosperity.

CuratorialText: Red is still associated with festivities and happiness in Chinese culture today. Learn more about Lunar New Year, when families decorate windows with red paper cuttings and adorn doors with couplets expressing auspicious wishes. Watch and learn how Chinese paper cuttings are made, from the Smithsonian Center for Folklife and Cultural Heritage.
persona2_adapted: In Chinese culture, red continues to symbolize celebration and joy. Discover more about Lunar New Year, a time when families place red paper cuttings on windows and hang couplets on doors to express good fortune. Observe the process of making Chinese paper cuttings, presented by the Smithsonian Center for Folklife and Cultural Heritage.

CuratorialText: Red can carry more negative associations as well, such as with warfare, rage, and destruction. The color of blood, there is a visceral quality to red that resonates uniquely with the human psyche. Haunted by the traces that the human body leaves behind, contemporary artist Chiharu Shiota’s 2015 installation “Over the Continents” amassed personal memories through an accumulation of nearly four hundred individual shoes. Collected by the artist, each shoe came with a note from the donor describing lost individuals and past moments. Almost four miles of red yarn wrapped around the shoes and attached to a single point in the museum’s Sackler pavilion, evoking the artist’s attention to the fundamental ties that connect humans to one another and the world.

CuratorialText: Whether it’s in the form of growing plants, precious stones, or deliberately blended pigments, there’s a human impulse to surround ourselves with green in the art we create, the environments we inhabit, and the spaces we build. Green evokes fertile landscapes, growth and prosperity, and even health and wellness. Learn about how green has been made and understood across various cultures and traditions represented in the museum’s collections.

Green suggests fertile landscapes, growth, prosperity, and even health and well-being. Explore how green has been produced and interpreted across different cultures and traditions represented in the museum’s collections.

CuratorialText: While there aren’t many plant sources of green dyes in the art world, some Indian paintings use beetle wings to mimic shiny green emeralds. In very special works of art, emeralds could be carved to make unique objects, or were made into jewelry.
persona2_adapted: While green plant-based dyes are rare in art, certain Indian paintings incorporate beetle wings to imitate the reflective quality of green emeralds. In exceptional works, emeralds were carved into one-of-a-kind objects or set into jewelry.

CuratorialText: The green patina on copper and bronze objects occurs as the copper corrodes. Patina occurs naturally when copper comes into contact with salty or acidic surroundings. See, for instance, how a range of corrosion products affects the surface color of the ancient Chinese bronze ritual vessel below: most of the surface is covered with a smooth, tin oxide-stained pale green with copper compounds, but there are also numerous scattered small patches of dull red cuprite, and the bright, powdery green seen here and there represents copper chloride. Explore this object in 3D.

However, there are also many small, scattered patches of dull red cuprite. The bright, powdery green seen in some areas is copper chloride. Explore this object in 3D.

CuratorialText: Copper corrosion products can be taken advantage of to create man-made copper green pigments; more commonly, however, the green mineral malachite can be ground to make paints. Malachite is often used in paintings in China.

CuratorialText:
Fine-ground malachite. Image courtesy of Conservation and Scientific Research, National Museum of Asian Art, Smithsonian Institution
Copper, together with lead, is often the basis of green glazes in ceramics. Mixtures of copper with yellow particles of lead tin oxide or lead antimony oxide are also used for green enamels.

Copper, together with lead, is often the base material for green glazes in ceramics. Mixtures of copper with yellow particles—such as lead tin oxide or lead antimony oxide—are also used to create green enamels.

CuratorialText: “Celadon” refers to a large family of high-fired ceramics. Their glazes are colored by small percentages of iron oxide. When fired in an oxygen-deprived, or reducing, atmosphere, the glazes turn variable shades of bluish green, traditionally described with the French word céladon. The word is commonly believed to have been derived from a seventeenth-century French drama, L’Astrée, in which a shepherd named Céladon appears dressed in a gray-green robe. Some modern scholars prefer the term “green ware,” but the poetic French name is more likely to remind a reader that celadon ceramics can exhibit a broad range of colors, from sky blue to sea green.
persona2_adapted:
**Adapted Text:**

"Celadon" describes a large group of high-fired ceramics. Their glazes contain small amounts of iron oxide, which gives them color. When these glazes are fired in an atmosphere without oxygen—called a reducing atmosphere—they turn into different shades of bluish green. This color is traditionally called by the French word *céladon*.

It is commonly believed that the word comes from a 17th-century French play, *L’Astrée*. In this play, a shepherd named Céladon appears wearing a gray-green robe.

Some modern scholars prefer the term "green ware." However, the poetic French name is more likely to remind a reader that celadon ceramics can show a wide range of colors—from sky blue to sea green.

CuratorialText: Jade was the first true luxury medium to emerge in ancient China. Although the term is used to refer to a range of hard, beautifully figured stones, most Chinese Neolithic, Bronze Age, and early imperial period examples have been scientifically identified as nephrite, geologically different from the bright, apple green jadeite used in much later times. Composed of interlocked, fibrous crystals and occurring as pebbles and boulders in a range of colors (due to iron, chromium, manganese, or similar materials fused in the rock), nephrite is an incredibly hard stone. It cannot be carved or even scratched by metal but instead must be ground into shape using quartz and other tough minerals. These qualities and its rarity inspired ancient Chinese people to value it very highly and to reserve its use for special purposes, such as for jewelry and ceremonial and ritual objects.
persona2_adapted:
Jade was the first true luxury material to appear in ancient China. Although the term "jade" is used for a range of hard, beautifully patterned stones, most examples from the Chinese Neolithic, Bronze Age, and early imperial periods have been scientifically identified as nephrite. This is geologically different from the bright, apple-green jadeite used in much later times.

Nephrite is made of interlocked, fibrous crystals. It occurs as pebbles and boulders in a variety of colors. These colors come from iron, chromium, manganese, or similar materials fused into the rock. Nephrite is an extremely hard stone. It cannot be carved or even scratched by metal. Instead, it must be ground into shape using quartz and other tough minerals.

Because of these qualities and its rarity, ancient Chinese people valued nephrite very highly. They reserved its use for special purposes, such as jewelry and ceremonial or ritual objects.

CuratorialText: According to his close friend Agnes Meyer, when Charles Lang Freer was ill near the end of his life, he often held a piece of jade in his hand, believing with an almost religious faith in the comforting and restorative powers of the stone. Freer collected ancient Chinese jades in large numbers, when most other Western collectors showed little interest. Ultimately, there were more than 500 pieces in his collection, spanning more than 5,000 years.

He believed, with an almost religious faith, that the stone had comforting and restorative powers. Freer collected ancient Chinese jades in large numbers, at a time when most other Western collectors showed little interest in them. CuratorialText: Green is so prevalent in the flora of the natural world that the color easily conjures lush environments teeming with plants. Life, growth, and regeneration are all concepts that come to mind in the context of verdant landscapes. In Southeast Asia, during the ninth through the thirteenth century, constructing cities required carving out spaces from the verdant, tropical environment. The jungle had to be systematically cultivated; water had to be diverted into reservoirs, moats, and canals; and the landscape had to be carefully tended. When population centers shifted due to political, ecological, and religious changes, the tropical landscape readily encroached on the built environment, transforming once urban landscapes back into jungle. This environment informed Southeast Asian artists: on temple walls and sculptures, plants and flowers grow on thick stalks, vines curlicue, and sprays of foliage transform surfaces into lush landscapes.
persona2_adapted: Green is so common in the natural world’s plant life that the color easily makes us think of lush environments full of vegetation. Life, growth, and regeneration are all ideas that come to mind when we see green landscapes. In Southeast Asia, between the ninth and thirteenth centuries, building cities required clearing spaces from the green, tropical environment. The jungle had to be systematically managed; water had to be directed into reservoirs, moats, and canals; and the landscape had to be carefully maintained. When population centers moved due to political, ecological, and religious changes, the tropical landscape quickly reclaimed the built environment, turning once-urban areas back into jungle. This environment influenced Southeast Asian artists: on temple walls and sculptures, plants and flowers grow on thick stalks, vines curl in spirals, and sprays of foliage cover surfaces, creating lush landscapes.

CuratorialText: Set in a garden, the image depicts a group of angels, referred to as huris or paris in Persian, entertaining their queen by playing music, dancing, and serving food and wine. The drawing's lively mood echoes other Persian representations of outdoor banquets or bazm (a form of celebration with wine and music) ceremonies, with the exception that this fine drawing features heavenly creatures--a favorite subject matter in sixteenth-and seventeenth-century Iran and Turkey--instead of the princely figures seen in illustrations to lyrical poetry by Hafiz and Assar.
persona2_adapted: Set in a garden, the image shows a group of angels—called *huris* or *paris* in Persian—entertaining their queen. They do so by playing music, dancing, and serving food and wine. The drawing’s lively mood is similar to other Persian representations of outdoor banquets or *bazm* ceremonies (a type of celebration with wine and music). However, in this fine drawing, the figures are heavenly creatures instead of princely figures. Heavenly creatures were a favorite subject in sixteenth- and seventeenth-century Iran and Turkey. Princely figures, by contrast, appear in illustrations to lyrical poetry by Hafiz and Assar.

CuratorialText: Referred to as peri winged celestial creatures are among some of the most popular subjects in sixteenth-century Ottoman and Persian drawings and paintings. The exquisitely rendered composition typifies a distinct style of Ottoman drawing that combines sweeping, calligraphic lines with soft, feathery strokes, as is evident in the minute designs on the figure's robe, cup, and bottle. Light washes of color add a sense of volume and texture to the form of this heavenly being.

This is visible in the minute designs on the figure's robe, cup, and bottle. Light washes of color add a sense of volume and texture to the form of this heavenly being.

CuratorialText: Four-part inro in form of flattened rectangle with rounded corners (probably derived from the coin called ryo). The four components are secured by a blue-green silk cord passing through two holes in each of the elements, tied in a bow below and secured by the bead (ojime) above and atttached to the toggle (netsuke) at the end.

CuratorialText: Box, small cylindrical, with cover of equal size with the box proper. Clay: hard, grayish. Glaze: pale greenish-gray celadon, with faint lilac tints. Decoration: inlaid in white and black under glaze: medallion of two flying cranes surrounded by geometric borders.

CuratorialText: A straight-sided cup which angles inward about three-quarters of the way toward a flattened bottom. The walls of the cup are very thin and this type of ware has been referred to as egg-shell ware. The fabric is a pinkish beige color. The inside of the cup is unpainted. The details on the outside of the cup are painted in brownish-black and red on an upainted background. The same motif of a red lotus bud shape outlined in brownish-black and tied with ribbons of the same brownish-black color is repeated seven times around the cup. The ornament color is repeated seven times around the cup. The ornament extends from the edge of the cup to about the middle of the cup. Within the cup are remains of a black powdery substance.

CuratorialText: This is one of several paintings of shop fronts that Whistler completed while in St. Ives. Sympathetic critics singled it out for enthusiastic praise when Whistler exhibited it in London in 1884. One declared, it "is probably the most perfect little thing of its kind that was ever wrought by an artist who has learnt how to see….It is without detail, without apparent labour, without dramatic interest; but it is exquisite in colour, faultless in tone, and its well-considered mystery has, at least, the interest of suggestiveness."
persona2_adapted: This is one of several paintings of shop fronts that Whistler made while in St. Ives. When Whistler showed it in London in 1884, sympathetic critics chose it for enthusiastic praise. One critic wrote: "It is probably the most perfect small work of its kind ever created by an artist who has learned how to see…. It has no detail, no visible effort, no dramatic interest. But it is exquisite in color, faultless in tone, and its well-considered mystery has, at least, the interest of suggestiveness."

CuratorialText:

Greek letters on label: (^o^) and No. 48.

CuratorialText: Molded Yue-ware vessels like this were made in large quantities, but their function is unclear. They may have held a candle, or perhaps scholars dipped their writing brushes in the water-filled interior. In the Jin dynasty (265-420 CE), mythical lions, with exaggeratedly curled manes, and winged chimeras were protective images; huge stone examples guarded important tombs.
persona2_adapted: Molded Yue-ware vessels like this were produced in large quantities, but their function remains uncertain. They may have held a candle. Alternatively, scholars might have dipped their writing brushes into the water-filled interior. During the Jin dynasty (265–420 CE), mythical lions—with exaggeratedly curled manes—and winged chimeras served as protective images. Large stone versions of these creatures guarded important tombs.

CuratorialText: A pair of blue and white molded porcelain beaker vases [F1992.47.1 and .2] decorated with freely drawn feather, foliate scroll and scattered flowerheads, each with a leaf or conch shell "emblem" mark on base. Part of a five-piece garniture set with three jars.

Each vase has a leaf or conch shell "emblem" mark on its base. These vases are part of a five-piece garniture set, which also includes three jars.

CuratorialText:
The Kiyotaki Waterfall is located along the route of the Tokaido, the great road passing through the eastern provinces of Japan and linking the city of Edo (now Tokyo), seat of the Tokugawa shogunate, with Kyoto, the capital and home of the emperor.
In this print from the series, Shokoku taki meguri (Famous Waterfalls in Various Provinces), the Kiyotaki Waterfall descends in branching rivulets over a sheer, rock face. In the foreground, pilgrims ascend from a teahouse toward a shrine dedicated to Kannon, a Buddhist deity associated with compassion. The remoteness and altitude of this scene is emphasized by the bands of clouds at the lower left.
persona2_adapted:
The Kiyotaki Waterfall is located along the route of the Tokaido. The Tokaido was a major road that passed through the eastern provinces of Japan. It connected the city of Edo (now Tokyo), which was the seat of the Tokugawa shogunate, with Kyoto, the capital and home of the emperor.

This print belongs to the series *Shokoku taki meguri* (Famous Waterfalls in Various Provinces). In the image, the Kiyotaki Waterfall flows down a steep, rock face in branching rivulets. In the foreground, pilgrims are climbing up from a teahouse toward a shrine. The shrine is dedicated to Kannon, a Buddhist deity associated with compassion. The scene feels remote and high up. This effect is reinforced by the bands of clouds in the lower left corner.

CuratorialText: Small dish with pleated rim. Clay: buff pottery. Glaze: transparent glaze with olive greenish tone (Ofuke glaze), large irregular cracks stained brown; glaze deeper green in tone where thick. Interior of footrim glazed; glazed wiped off outside of footrim and surrounding base. Decoration: pleating on narrow everted rim. The form of this dish with fluted rim distantly echoes the shape of Chinese Longquan-ware celadon dishes.

Small dish with pleated rim.

Glaze: transparent glaze with an olive-greenish tone (Ofuke glaze). The glaze has large, irregular cracks that are stained brown. The glaze is a deeper green where it is thicker.  
The inside of the footrim is glazed. The glaze was wiped off the outside of the footrim and the surrounding base.  
Decoration: pleating on the narrow, everted rim.  

The form of this dish, with its fluted rim, distantly echoes the shape of Chinese Longquan-ware celadon dishes.

CuratorialText:
The Khamsa, or Quintet, by Nizami ranks among the great masterpieces of Persian literature. The romance of Khosraw and Shirin, one of the five poems that constitute the Khamsa, narrates the adventures of the Sasanian king Khusraw Parviz and the Armenian princess Shirin. Attributed to the early fifteenth century, the Freer Khamsa is one of the earliest known illustrated copies of this work.
Khosraw at the Gate of Shirin's Castle is among the most poignantly expressive manuscript paintings of the fifteenth century. Displeased with Khosraw's behavior and his marriage to another woman, Shirin has withdrawn to her castle. Following the death of his queen, Khosraw repeatedly attempts to see Shirin, who finally yields to his request. Shielded by the royal umbrella and standing on precious brocades and carpets, the king gestures in supplication toward Shirin, who gazes down from a window in her castle. Although the text specifies that Khosraw visited Shirin at dawn in the dead of winter, the artist has situated the reconciliation in a lush garden setting softly illuminated by a waning moon. The elegant, mustachioed Khosraw in these paintings bears a striking resemblance to known depictions of the Timurid patron Baysunghur Mirza (1399-1434).
persona2_adapted:
The *Khamsa* (or *Quintet*) by Nizami is one of the great masterpieces of Persian literature. The romance of *Khosraw and Shirin* is one of the five poems that make up the *Khamsa*. It tells the story of the Sasanian king Khusraw Parviz and the Armenian princess Shirin. The Freer *Khamsa* is attributed to the early fifteenth century. It is one of the earliest known illustrated copies of this work.

*Khosraw at the Gate of Shirin’s Castle* is one of the most emotionally expressive manuscript paintings of the fifteenth century. Shirin is unhappy with Khosraw’s behavior and his marriage to another woman. Because of this, she has withdrawn to her castle. After the death of his queen, Khosraw tries many times to see Shirin. Finally, she agrees to his request. The king is protected by the royal umbrella and stands on precious brocades and carpets. He gestures in supplication toward Shirin, who looks down from a window in her castle. The text says that Khosraw visited Shirin at dawn in the middle of winter. However, the artist has placed the reconciliation in a lush garden setting, softly lit by a waning moon. In these paintings, the elegant, mustachioed Khosraw looks very similar to known depictions of the Timurid patron Baysunghur Mirza (1399–1434).

CuratorialText: Set in the London home of Whistler's brother-in-law Seymour Haden, this painting was originally titled The Morning Call. The woman reflected in the mirror at the left is Whistler's half sister Deborah Haden. The seated girl is his niece, Annie Haden. The standing woman, Miss Isabella Boott, was connected to the Haden family by marriage. Instead of trying to create a convincing illusion of three-dimensional space, Whistler adopted a color scheme and sharply angled perspective that emphasize the flatness of the picture plane.
persona2_adapted:
Set in the London home of Whistler's brother-in-law Seymour Haden, this painting was originally titled *The Morning Call*.  
The woman reflected in the mirror on the left is Whistler's half-sister, Deborah Haden.  
The seated girl is his niece, Annie Haden.  
The standing woman, Miss Isabella Boott, was connected to the Haden family through marriage.  

Rather than attempting to create a convincing illusion of three-dimensional space, Whistler used a color scheme and a sharply angled perspective that emphasize the flatness of the picture plane.

CuratorialText: Popular tales and lively images played a role in transmitting beliefs and values to the Jain community. The Shripalarasa is a narrative that recounts the wondrous adventures of the Jain Prince Shripala, who travels to distant lands in search of trade and wealth. In this unusually large and vivacious painting, the artist represents the Prince's ship as a British vessel. The inscriptions scattered across the painting, which repetively identify parts of the ship, indicate a fascination with seafaring. The depiction of the Union Jack lends immediacy to a story that took place long before the British came to India.

CuratorialText: Funeral vase: tall, slender, on hollowed, spreading feet. Wood stand. Clay: soft, light-weight, gray-white. Glaze: thin, transparent green celadon; close brown crackle. Areas of stain and of over-baked wrinkled glaze. Decoration: hieratic figures and a dragon supporting the sun-disk - in applied relief, under glaze.
