He applied Linnaeus's system to Sloane's collections and to the natural history specimens that Solander himself collected with Sir Joseph Banks during the first Cook voyage. Many of these original specimens are included in the exhibition, along with a number of fossils. By 1880, there were so many natural history specimens that they needed their own museum—the Natural History Museum in South Kensington.

CuratorialText: Antiquaries collected books, manuscripts, drawings and artefacts from the past, and travelled the country studying ruins in order to learn more about Britain's Roman past and its earlier history. It was fairly easy to learn about medieval times because many of these objects had survived, including religious artefacts, armour, books and buildings. But antiquaries also began to survey and map Roman and earlier sites, such as Stonehenge, in a more scientific and systematic way and to learn more about the earliest Britons.  This knowledge, combined with the new study of rock strata, led archaeologists to question the accepted date of the world's creation – 4004 BC – calculated from readings of the Bible.
persona2_adapted: Antiquaries collected books, manuscripts, drawings, and artefacts from the past. They also traveled across the country to study ruins. Their goal was to learn more about Britain’s Roman past and its earlier history. It was fairly easy to learn about medieval times because many objects from that period had survived. These included religious artefacts, armour, books, and buildings. However, antiquaries also began to survey and map Roman and even earlier sites—such as Stonehenge—in a more scientific and systematic way. Through this work, they sought to learn more about the earliest Britons. This knowledge, combined with the new study of rock strata, led archaeologists to question the accepted date of the world’s creation: 4004 BC. That date had been calculated from readings of the Bible.

CuratorialText: In 1824, Charles Townley's famous collection of Greek and Roman sculptures, acquired on his Grand Tours in Italy, came to the British Museum. They joined the collections of prints and drawings, gems, coins, bronzes, vases and other classical antiquities from fellow connoisseurs such as Sir William Hamilton and Richard Payne Knight. These men studied each other's collections to learn about the 'progress' of art, from 'primitive' beginnings in early civilisations to what they considered to be the height of artistic achievement – the sculpture and architecture of classical Greece. This became the standard against which to measure all art and led, in late 18th-century Britain, to a classical revival in architecture and the decorative arts.
persona2_adapted:
In 1824, the British Museum acquired Charles Townley’s famous collection of Greek and Roman sculptures. Townley had gathered these works during his Grand Tours in Italy. The collection joined existing holdings of prints, drawings, gems, coins, bronzes, vases, and other classical antiquities. These had come from fellow connoisseurs, including Sir William Hamilton and Richard Payne Knight.

These men studied each other’s collections to understand what they saw as the “progress” of art. They believed art moved from “primitive” beginnings in early civilizations to its highest achievement: the sculpture and architecture of classical Greece. For them, this classical art became the standard against which all other art was measured. In late 18th-century Britain, this idea led to a classical revival in architecture and the decorative arts.

CuratorialText: Everything gathered in the 18th century had to be classified and organised so that it could be more easily understood and used.  King George III's library was presented to the nation by George IV and was housed in the room that is now the Enlightenment Gallery (his library is now in the British Library). The coins and medals collection was among the first part of the library to arrive at the Museum and they were arranged by country and ruler to help bring past history alive. George III was also keenly interested in the navy, navigation and scientific discoveries and collected instruments and working models. Sir William Hamilton arranged his vases found in the area around Naples according to the styles of painting, attempting to establish a chronology for them. He later published them in beautifully illustrated folios. In the early part of the century, Sir Hans Sloane filled his cabinets with curious 'artificial' (manmade) objects from around the world, including those collected from enslaved peoples in Jamaica. These objects were catalogued and displayed in many different ways – by type, material, date or purpose.
persona2_adapted: Everything collected in the 18th century had to be classified and organized so that it could be more easily understood and used. King George III’s library was given to the nation by George IV and placed in the room that is now the Enlightenment Gallery (his library is currently in the British Library). The collection of coins and medals was among the first part of the library to arrive at the Museum. These objects were arranged by country and ruler, in order to make past history feel more alive. George III was also very interested in the navy, navigation, and scientific discoveries. He collected instruments and working models. Sir William Hamilton arranged his vases—found in the area around Naples—according to painting styles, trying to establish a chronology for them. Later, he published them in beautifully illustrated folios. In the early part of the century, Sir Hans Sloane filled his cabinets with curious “artificial” (manmade) objects from around the world. These included objects collected from enslaved people in Jamaica. These objects were catalogued and displayed in many different ways—by type, material, date, or purpose.

CuratorialText: The Enlightenment led to a fevered interest in deciphering the mysterious forms of Egyptian hieroglyphs and the cuneiform inscriptions found in lands described in the Bible as well as early forms of Sanskrit in India. Scholars 'cracked the codes' in the 19th century, opening up thousands of years of history. At the same time, antiquaries deciphered runes and other early forms of writing, revealing more of our own history. This was a period when significant numbers of antiquities, acquired from collectors, diplomats and through excavation, entered the British Museum.
persona2_adapted: The Enlightenment sparked an intense interest in understanding the mysterious forms of Egyptian hieroglyphs, the cuneiform inscriptions from lands mentioned in the Bible, and early forms of Sanskrit in India. In the 19th century, scholars deciphered these writing systems. This opened up thousands of years of history. At the same time, antiquaries decoded runes and other early scripts. This revealed more of our own history. During this period, a large number of antiquities entered the British Museum. They came from collectors, diplomats, and through excavation.

CuratorialText: Christian mistrust of superstition and idols, rituals and magic led to ignorance about other religions. Enlightenment scholars attempted to dispel this by studying the ancient and modern religions they encountered. They researched rituals and cults in different cultures, collected religious artefacts, grouped gods into families and examined their attributes and functions. They wanted to find similarities that might shed light on the origins of the world's religions. Much of this activity took place within the context of colonialism.
persona2_adapted: Christian mistrust of superstition, idols, rituals, and magic caused a lack of knowledge about other religions. Enlightenment scholars tried to correct this by studying the ancient and modern religions they encountered. They researched rituals and cults in different cultures, collected religious objects, organized gods into families, and examined their attributes and functions. Their goal was to find similarities that might explain the origins of the world's religions. Much of this work happened within the context of colonialism.

CuratorialText: In this era of colonialism and global trade, travellers, traders, scientists, explorers and diplomats created collections of ethnographic objects, including ceremonial and everyday items, thought representative of customs and cultures of people around the globe. They collected artefacts and published illustrated accounts about the lives and cultures of people around the world, from a European perspective. Alongside Sir Hans Sloane's collection, the British Museum also houses objects from the voyages of Captain James Cook and Sir Joseph Banks to Australia, New Zealand, Tahiti and other Pacific islands. A significant number of objects were acquired through employees of the East India and Royal Africa Companies, whose wealth was made possible through the use of enslaved labour and the exploitation of people and resources.
persona2_adapted:
In this period of colonialism and global trade, travelers, traders, scientists, explorers, and diplomats assembled collections of ethnographic objects. These included both ceremonial and everyday items. People believed these objects represented the customs and cultures of different groups around the world. The collectors gathered artifacts and published illustrated reports about the lives and cultures of people worldwide, always from a European point of view.

Alongside Sir Hans Sloane's collection, the British Museum also holds objects from the voyages of Captain James Cook and Sir Joseph Banks. These voyages took them to Australia, New Zealand, Tahiti, and other Pacific islands. A large number of objects were acquired through employees of the East India Company and the Royal Africa Company. The wealth of these companies was made possible by the use of enslaved labor and the exploitation of people and resources.

CuratorialText: During his leadership, from 336–323 BC, Alexander the Great conquered the Greek city-states, the Persian Empire and founded more than 70 cities creating an empire that stretched across three continents and covered about two million square miles.  The Hellenistic period dates from the death of Alexander the Great in 323 BC to the defeat of Cleopatra VII and Mark Antony at Actium in 31 BC. After his death, Alexander's empire was divided up into kingdoms, ruled by his generals. The major dynasties sponsored major architectural programmes and were great patrons of the arts and culture. Objects on display in Room 22 include a colossal carved column drum from the Temple of Artemis at Ephesos (one of the Seven Wonders of the Ancient World), the Demeter from Knidos in south-west Turkey and Greek bronzes. Displays of smaller objects show the skill of ancient goldsmiths, metalworkers, potters and coroplasts (makers of clay or terracotta figurines) and gem cutters.
persona2_adapted: During his rule, from 336 to 323 BC, Alexander the Great conquered the Greek city-states and the Persian Empire. He also founded more than 70 cities. In doing so, he created an empire that stretched across three continents and covered about two million square miles. The Hellenistic period begins with the death of Alexander the Great in 323 BC and ends with the defeat of Cleopatra VII and Mark Antony at Actium in 31 BC. After Alexander died, his empire was divided into kingdoms. These were ruled by his generals. The major dynasties sponsored large architectural projects and were important patrons of the arts and culture. Objects on display in Room 22 include a colossal carved column drum from the Temple of Artemis at Ephesos—one of the Seven Wonders of the Ancient World—the Demeter from Knidos in south-west Turkey, and Greek bronzes. Displays of smaller objects show the skill of ancient goldsmiths, metalworkers, potters, coroplasts (makers of clay or terracotta figurines), and gem cutters.

CuratorialText: The exhibition is a dazzling celebration of the artistry and history of Hawaiʻi. It brings together remarkable objects – from feathered cloaks worn by chiefs and finely carved deities, to powerful shark-toothed weapons and bold contemporary works by Kānaka ʻŌiwi (Native Hawaiian) artists. In 1824, the young King Liholiho and Queen Kamāmalu travelled across vast oceans on a journey that would mark a turning point in the history of their kingdom, Hawaiʻi. The exhibition commemorates over 200 years since this ill-fated royal visit and sheds light on Hawaiʻi's history and culture through an exploration of the complex and enduring relationship between the Hawaiian and British nations.

The exhibition commemorates over 200 years since this ill-fated royal visit. It sheds light on Hawaiʻi's history and culture. It does so by exploring the complex and enduring relationship between the Hawaiian and British nations.

CuratorialText: This fashion show presents contemporary clothing of the Moana-Nui-a-Kiwa (the great ocean of Kiwa/the Pacific Ocean), which has been facilitated in the UK by Interisland Collective and curated by Lyall Hakaraia. The collection has been conceived and made by Māori designer Lyall Hakaraia in collaboration with venerated practitioners from across the Pacific. These practictioners are tohunga (experts) who are building and continue to grow Pacific cultural knowledge. They consider themselves as guides and strive to be 'good ancestors' through their daily practices, powerful intentions and vision. They are building resilient futures for mokopuna (grandchildren) and generations to come. The show will act as a focal point for the Pacific diaspora community as they activate a VĀ (portal) to their ancestors. The show is an offering to those ancestors that remain at the British Museum and are embodied through Pacific collections. It aims to 'warm' them in slumber. The collective will sing, chant and offer prayers to them during the show with Aroha (love) from across the Pacific.
persona2_adapted:
This fashion show presents contemporary clothing from Moana-Nui-a-Kiwa (the great ocean of Kiwa / the Pacific Ocean). The event has been organized in the UK by Interisland Collective and curated by Lyall Hakaraia.

The collection was conceived and made by Māori designer Lyall Hakaraia. He worked in collaboration with venerated practitioners from across the Pacific. These practitioners are tohunga (experts) who are building and continuing to grow Pacific cultural knowledge. They see themselves as guides. They strive to be 'good ancestors' through their daily practices, powerful intentions, and vision. They are building resilient futures for mokopuna (grandchildren) and for generations to come.

The show will act as a focal point for the Pacific diaspora community. Through the show, they will activate a VĀ (portal) to their ancestors. The show is an offering to those ancestors who remain at the British Museum and are embodied through Pacific collections. Its aim is to 'warm' them in their slumber. During the show, the collective will sing, chant, and offer prayers to them with Aroha (love) from across the Pacific.

CuratorialText: One half of the gallery presents the histories of China from 5000 BC to the present: from iconic Ming dynasty blue-and-white porcelain to delicate handscrolls, from magnificent Tang dynasty tomb figurines to modern works of art. The displays feature the richness of art and material culture in China, including painting, prints, jade, bronze, lacquer and ceramics.
persona2_adapted: One half of the gallery presents the history of China from 5000 BC to the present. It includes iconic Ming dynasty blue-and-white porcelain, delicate handscrolls, magnificent Tang dynasty tomb figurines, and modern works of art. The displays show the richness of art and material culture in China. This includes painting, prints, jade, bronze, lacquer, and ceramics.

CuratorialText: The other half of the gallery presents South Asia's many histories chronologically and by region, from early human occupation to the present. Highlights include seals from the Indus civilisation, superb south Indian sculptures of Shiva and one of the finest statues of the goddess Tara from Sri Lanka. Sophisticated paintings and objects from the courts of the Mughal emperors can be seen alongside 20th-century paintings, including by the Nobel laureate Rabindranath Tagore.
persona2_adapted: The other half of the gallery presents South Asia's many histories in chronological order and by region, from early human occupation to the present. Highlights include seals from the Indus civilisation, superb south Indian sculptures of Shiva, and one of the finest statues of the goddess Tara from Sri Lanka. Sophisticated paintings and objects from the courts of the Mughal emperors are displayed alongside 20th-century paintings, including works by the Nobel laureate Rabindranath Tagore.

CuratorialText: China is one of the world's oldest civilisations. Today it covers a vast territory the size of Europe and is home to a quarter of the world's population. China has produced a highly distinctive culture with beautifully crafted objects made on an industrial scale from the earliest times. Room 33 examines the past 7,000 years of China's history, exploring the themes of writing systems, rituals, beliefs, war, international trade and more. Room 33 also explores South Asia's history, from 1.5 million years ago to the present day. Trade, exchange, migration and political power are some of the themes examined. South Asia spans India, Pakistan, Bangladesh, Bhutan, Nepal, the Tibetan plateau and Sri Lanka. The region is home to numerous ancient and modern languages, such as Sanskrit, Tamil, Hindi and Urdu. The Buddhist, Hindu, Jain, Sikh and Islamic faiths have inspired magnificent architecture, sculpture, painting, literature and music.
persona2_adapted:
China is one of the world’s oldest civilizations. Today, it covers a vast territory—comparable in size to Europe—and is home to one-quarter of the global population. China developed a highly distinctive culture, producing beautifully crafted objects on an industrial scale from the earliest periods. Room 33 examines the past 7,000 years of Chinese history, exploring themes such as writing systems, rituals, beliefs, warfare, international trade, and more.

Room 33 also presents the history of South Asia, from 1.5 million years ago to the present day. Key themes include trade, exchange, migration, and political power. South Asia includes India, Pakistan, Bangladesh, Bhutan, Nepal, the Tibetan Plateau, and Sri Lanka. This region is home to many ancient and modern languages, such as Sanskrit, Tamil, Hindi, and Urdu. The Buddhist, Hindu, Jain, Sikh, and Islamic faiths have inspired magnificent architecture, sculpture, painting, literature, and music.

CuratorialText:
Africa's rich cultural heritage and histories are represented at the Museum both in Africa (Room 25), The Sainsbury Galleries, and across the galleries. Join our volunteer-led tours, developed in collaboration with community members, to explore their personal selection of objects ranging from the ancient world to the present day. Each guide will deliver their own personal tour exploring one of the following themes:
Out of Africa: stories of migration, materials and mythology
The Igbo worldview: artefacts from the past in the British Museum
African metal artefacts: tradition, technique and trade
Ancient objects from African kingdoms
persona2_adapted:
Africa's rich cultural heritage and histories are represented at the Museum in three locations: Africa (Room 25), The Sainsbury Galleries, and across other galleries. We offer volunteer-led tours, developed together with community members. These tours allow you to explore the guides' personal selection of objects, from the ancient world to the present day. Each guide will present their own personal tour, focusing on one of the following themes:

- Out of Africa: stories of migration, materials, and mythology  
- The Igbo worldview: artefacts from the past in the British Museum  
- African metal artefacts: tradition, technique, and trade  
- Ancient objects from African kingdoms

CuratorialText:
Step into spring and the month of May in this creative play session for children aged five and under.
Meet May Queens, Green Men and woodland creatures in this child-led, open-ended creative session. Stop and smell some floral scents, draw and scribble with a range of art materials and make crowns and masks to wear for spring festivities! Working at your child's pace, together we'll explore textures, colours and scents through a range of materials.
persona2_adapted:
Step into spring and the month of May in this creative play session for children aged five and under.

Encounter May Queens, Green Men, and woodland creatures in this child-led, open-ended creative session. Pause to smell floral scents, draw and scribble with a variety of art materials, and create crowns and masks to wear for spring festivities. Working at your child's pace, together we will explore textures, colours, and scents through a range of materials.

CuratorialText: Sennacherib was the king of Assyria from 704–681 BC and was famous for his building projects. The rooms and courtyards of his Neo-Assyrian Southwest Palace at Nineveh were decorated with a series of detailed carved stone panels. Many of these stone panels are on display in Room 9. The panels depict a variety of scenes, including the transport of huge sculptures of human-headed winged bulls (lamassu) that weigh up to 30 tons and were intended for the main entrances to the palace. These illustrations provide an insight into ancient quarrying and transport techniques, as well as Sennacherib's keen interest in his building projects. Other panels on display depict the king's military campaigns.

The rooms and courtyards of his Neo-Assyrian Southwest Palace at Nineveh were decorated with a series of detailed carved stone panels. Many of these stone panels are on display in Room 9. The panels depict a variety of scenes, including the transport of huge sculptures of human-headed winged bulls (lamassu) that weigh up to 30 tons and were intended for the main entrances to the palace. These illustrations provide insight into ancient quarrying and transport techniques, as well as Sennacherib's strong interest in his building projects. Other panels on display depict the king's military campaigns.

CuratorialText: Split across three rooms, these galleries display life during this period in Assyrian culture. On display are lion hunting reliefs, the siege of the city of Lachish and scenes from inside the palace of King Sargon II at Khorsabad.  Visitors to Room 10a are greeted by a pair of colossal human-headed winged bulls. Intended as a supernatural guardians, these gigantic figures guarded the palace of King Sargon II at Khorsabad, and were intended to deter evil of all kinds from entering.

**Adapted Text:**

These galleries are spread across three rooms. They show life in Assyrian culture during this period. On display are lion hunting reliefs, the siege of the city of Lachish, and scenes from inside the palace of King Sargon II at Khorsabad.

In Room 10a, visitors are greeted by a pair of colossal human-headed winged bulls. These gigantic figures were intended as supernatural guardians. They guarded the palace of King Sargon II at Khorsabad. Their purpose was to deter all kinds of evil from entering.

CuratorialText: In ancient Assyria, hunting lions was considered the sport of kings, symbolic of the ruling monarch's duty to protect and fight for his people. The sculpted reliefs in Room 10a illustrate the sporting exploits of the last great Assyrian king, Ashurbanipal (668–631 BC) and were created for his palace at Nineveh (in modern-day northern Iraq). The hunt scenes, full of tension and realism, rank among the finest achievements of Assyrian Art. They depict the release of the lions, the ensuing chase and subsequent killing.
persona2_adapted: In ancient Assyria, hunting lions was considered the sport of kings. It symbolized the ruling monarch’s duty to protect and fight for his people. The sculpted reliefs in Room 10a illustrate the sporting exploits of the last great Assyrian king, Ashurbanipal (668–631 BC). These reliefs were created for his palace at Nineveh (in modern-day northern Iraq). The hunt scenes are full of tension and realism. They rank among the finest achievements of Assyrian Art. The scenes depict the release of the lions, the chase that follows, and the subsequent killing.

CuratorialText: Lachish was one of the chief cities of the kingdom of Judah in the southern Levant and in 701 BC it was captured by the Assyrian King Sennacherib (704–681 BC). The siege followed the refusal of Lachish to pay tribute to the Assyrian Empire (based in modern northern Iraq) and is mentioned in the Bible. Many of the relief sculptures on display in Room 10b depict the capture of the city, alongside a selection of items and weaponry used in the siege. A 'prism' inscribed with an Assyrian account of the campaign is also on show.
persona2_adapted:
Lachish was one of the main cities of the kingdom of Judah, located in the southern Levant. In 701 BC, it was captured by the Assyrian king Sennacherib (reigned 704–681 BC). The siege occurred because Lachish refused to pay tribute to the Assyrian Empire, which was based in what is now northern Iraq. This event is mentioned in the Bible.

Many of the relief sculptures displayed in Room 10b depict the capture of the city. Also on view are a selection of objects and weapons used during the siege. Additionally, a 'prism' inscribed with an Assyrian account of the campaign is exhibited.

CuratorialText: The city and palace at Khorsabad (in modern northern Iraq), was built for the Assyrian King Sargon II (721–705 BC). The palace entrances were originally dominated by pairs of colossal human-headed winged bulls, which were intended as guardians, accompanied by protective spirits with magical powers. Two of these impressive statues now stand in Room 10c, along with carvings depicting the king and crown prince, royal courtiers and hunting scenes. Inscriptions on display in the gallery come from a similar winged bull from the palace of Sennacherib (704–681 BC) at nearby Nineveh and were badly burnt when the city was destroyed in 612 BC.
persona2_adapted: The city and palace at Khorsabad (in modern northern Iraq) were built for the Assyrian King Sargon II (721–705 BC). The palace entrances were originally dominated by pairs of colossal human-headed winged bulls. These figures served as guardians and were accompanied by protective spirits with magical powers. Two of these impressive statues are now displayed in Room 10c. They are joined by carvings that show the king, the crown prince, royal courtiers, and hunting scenes. The inscriptions on display in the gallery come from a similar winged bull. That bull was part of the palace of Sennacherib (704–681 BC) at nearby Nineveh. These inscriptions were badly burnt when the city was destroyed in 612 BC.

CuratorialText: Rooms 57–59 present the material culture of the region from the Neolithic farmers of the eighth millennium BC to the fall of the Neo-Babylonian Empire in 539 BC, within the context of major historical events. Objects on display illustrate the continuity of the Canaanite culture of the southern Levant throughout this period. They highlight the indigenous origins of both the Israelites and the Phoenicians. The display compares this culture with that of the people of central inland Syria, the Amorites and the Aramaeans.
persona2_adapted: Rooms 57–59 present the material culture of the region, from the Neolithic farmers of the eighth millennium BC to the fall of the Neo-Babylonian Empire in 539 BC. This is shown within the context of major historical events. The objects on display illustrate the continuity of Canaanite culture in the southern Levant throughout this period. They highlight the indigenous origins of both the Israelites and the Phoenicians. The display also compares this culture with that of the people of central inland Syria—the Amorites and the Aramaeans.

CuratorialText: Ancient Anatolia and Urartu form an important land link between Europe and Asia. They lie where the modern Republic of Turkey, Armenia, Georgia and north-west Iran are located today.  Examples of Early Bronze Age craftsmanship on display include silver bull and cup. Business archives of Middle Bronze Age merchants illustrate trading between central Anatolia and Mesopotamia (modern Iraq). Delicate gold jewellery and figurines date from the Hittite period. Iron Age objects from Urartu, include winged bulls and griffins used to decorate furniture.
persona2_adapted: Ancient Anatolia and Urartu served as a crucial land bridge between Europe and Asia. These regions correspond to the modern territories of Turkey, Armenia, Georgia, and northwestern Iran. Among the objects on display from the Early Bronze Age are a silver bull and a cup. Commercial archives from the Middle Bronze Age document trade between central Anatolia and Mesopotamia (present-day Iraq). Delicate gold jewelry and figurines date from the Hittite period. Iron Age artifacts from Urartu include winged bulls and griffins, which were used as furniture decorations.

CuratorialText: Reaching back more than 2,000 years, this exhibition explored the origins of Hindu, Jain and Buddhist sacred art in the ancient and powerful nature spirits of India, and the spread of this art beyond the subcontinent.  One of the first major exhibitions in the world to look at the early devotional art of India from a multi-faith, contemporary and global perspective, it highlighted the inspiration behind now-familiar depictions of the deities and enlightened teachers of these world religions – and how they were shared across the Indian Ocean to Southeast Asia and along the Silk Roads to East Asia.
persona2_adapted:
This exhibition examined the origins of Hindu, Jain, and Buddhist sacred art. It looked back more than 2,000 years to the ancient and powerful nature spirits of India. It also traced how this art spread beyond the Indian subcontinent.

This was one of the first major exhibitions in the world to study early devotional art of India from a multi-faith, contemporary, and global perspective. It highlighted what inspired the now-familiar depictions of deities and enlightened teachers in these world religions. It also showed how these images were shared across the Indian Ocean to Southeast Asia and along the Silk Roads to East Asia.

CuratorialText: Colourful, multi-sensory and atmospheric, this exhibition was developed in collaboration with an advisory community panel of practising Hindus, Buddhists and Jains. These living religious traditions and their sacred art are now integral to the daily lives of almost two billion people around the world including in the UK. Key loans from our community partners helped to tell this contemporary story.

**Adapted Text:**

This exhibition is colourful, multi-sensory, and atmospheric. It was developed in collaboration with an advisory community panel. This panel includes practising Hindus, Buddhists, and Jains.

These are living religious traditions. Their sacred art is now part of the daily lives of almost two billion people around the world, including in the UK. Important loans from our community partners helped to tell this contemporary story.

CuratorialText: The exhibition showcased more than 180 objects – including sculptures, paintings, drawings and manuscripts – from the South Asian collection at the British Museum as well as generous loans from national and international partners. It highlighted provenance, examining the stories, from creation to acquisition by museums, of every object in the show.
persona2_adapted: The exhibition presented over 180 objects—including sculptures, paintings, drawings, and manuscripts—from the British Museum’s South Asian collection, along with generous loans from national and international partners. It emphasized provenance, examining the journey of each object, from its creation to its acquisition by museums.

CuratorialText: From the symbolic footprints which preceded portrayals of the Buddha in human form to the cosmic serpents incorporated into Hindu art and the nature spirits who attend Jain enlightened teachers, this compelling exhibition told the ancient stories behind these living traditions.
persona2_adapted: From the symbolic footprints that came before images of the Buddha in human form, to the cosmic serpents included in Hindu art, and the nature spirits that accompany Jain enlightened teachers—this powerful exhibition presented the ancient stories behind these living traditions.

CuratorialText: The first exhibition on Hiroshige to be held at the British Museum, and the first on the artist in London for more than a quarter of a century, this was a visually stunning portrait of a country about to change forever. Born during an unsettled time in Japan's history, Utagawa Hiroshige (1797–1858) went on to become one of the country's most talented, prolific and popular artists. As Japan confronted the encroaching outside world, Hiroshige's calm artistic vision connected with – and reassured – people at every level of society. From fashionable figures and energetic city views to remote landscapes and impressions of the natural world, Hiroshige captured many aspects of life in the Japan of his time. Stunning bird-and-flower prints reveal his poetic feeling for nature while his evocative landscapes reflected the growing interest in travel across Japan. Hiroshige portrayed his world sometimes as it was, but often the way he imagined it could be.
persona2_adapted: The first exhibition on Hiroshige at the British Museum, and the first in London on this artist in over twenty-five years, presented a visually striking portrait of a country on the verge of profound change. Born during a period of instability in Japan's history, Utagawa Hiroshige (1797–1858) became one of the nation's most gifted, prolific, and beloved artists. As Japan faced increasing pressure from the outside world, Hiroshige's serene artistic vision resonated with—and offered reassurance to—people across all levels of society. From fashionable figures and dynamic city views to remote landscapes and depictions of the natural world, Hiroshige captured many facets of life in his contemporary Japan. His exquisite bird-and-flower prints reveal a poetic sensitivity to nature, while his evocative landscapes reflect the growing interest in travel throughout Japan. Hiroshige portrayed his world sometimes as it was, but often as he imagined it could be.

CuratorialText: Possessed of remarkable technical skills, both as a colourist and draftsman, Hiroshige had a sympathetic regard for people from all walks of life. Unlike most other print designers of his day, he came from a samurai family, but crossed social boundaries to devote himself to depicting popular customs. His work was affordable, too – along with celebrated landscape prints, he also designed hundreds of hand-held, disposable fans that were available to all.
persona2_adapted: Possessing remarkable technical skills as both a colorist and a draftsman, Hiroshige showed a sympathetic regard for people from all social classes. Unlike most other print designers of his time, he came from a samurai family, yet he crossed social boundaries to dedicate himself to depicting popular customs. His work was also affordable. In addition to his celebrated landscape prints, he designed hundreds of hand-held, disposable fans that were available to everyone.

CuratorialText: The exhibition featured prints, drawings, illustrated books and paintings from the British Museum collection, as well as a significant gift and loan of prints from Alan Medaugh, a major US collector of Hiroshige's work, and other important loans. As well as exploring Hiroshige's incredible body of work, this show considered his global legacy, which spans from Japan's Edo period (1615–1868) through to Vincent van Gogh and contemporary artists such as Julian Opie.
persona2_adapted: The exhibition included prints, drawings, illustrated books, and paintings from the British Museum collection. It also featured a major gift and loan of prints from Alan Medaugh, a prominent US collector of Hiroshige’s work, along with other important loans. In addition to examining Hiroshige’s remarkable oeuvre, the exhibition addressed his global legacy. This legacy extends from Japan’s Edo period (1615–1868) to Vincent van Gogh and contemporary artists such as Julian Opie.

CuratorialText: A stone figure of 'Hara-Pārvatī' (Śiva seated with Pārvatī on his knee). Also known as 'Umamahesvaramurti'. Accompanied by a lion and a bull (Nandi) and surrounded by many flying and standing attendants (musicians, garland-bearers), there is also a row of ritual equipment: two conch-shell trumpets on stands, a lamp and a bell.

CuratorialText: Head and upper body of pink/grey granite monumental statue of Ramses II (one of a pair placed before the door of the Ramesseum) wearing nemes head-cloth and circlet of uraei (about half now lost), the sculptor has exploited the bichrome nature of the stone to emphasise the division between body and face; the dorsal pillar is inscribed with vertical registers of hieroglyphs - giving the name and titles of the king and part of a dedication to Amun-Ra; in 1817 it was noted that there were traces of colour upon the statue and it may have, therefore, been painted red in antiquity.
persona2_adapted: Head and upper body of a pink/grey granite monumental statue of Ramses II (one of a pair originally placed before the door of the Ramesseum). The statue wears a *nemes* head-cloth and a circlet of *uraei* (about half of this is now lost). The sculptor used the bichrome nature of the stone to emphasize the division between body and face. The dorsal pillar is inscribed with vertical registers of hieroglyphs. These give the name and titles of the king, as well as part of a dedication to Amun-Ra. In 1817, it was noted that there were traces of color on the statue. Therefore, it may have been painted red in antiquity.

CuratorialText: Base and lid of the anthropoid wooden inner coffin of Hornedjitef, son of Nekhthorheb, who held a large number of priestly offices. Polychrome painted and gilded face, wig, collar and pectoral, winged scarab across breast, body inscribed with vertical registers of painted hieroglyphs, flanked by deities, the rest of the surface is unelaborated. Interior of lid decorated with a figure of Nut, surrounded by astronomical images of decan stars and constellations. Interior of base also decorated.
persona2_adapted:
**Base and lid of the anthropoid wooden inner coffin of Hornedjitef, son of Nekhthorheb, who held many priestly offices.**  
The face, wig, collar, and pectoral are painted in polychrome and gilded. A winged scarab appears across the chest. The body is inscribed with vertical registers of painted hieroglyphs, flanked by deities. The rest of the surface is left plain.  

The interior of the lid is decorated with a figure of Nut, surrounded by astronomical images of decan stars and constellations. The interior of the base is also decorated.

CuratorialText: Brass (heavily leaded zinc-brass) head cast using the lost wax (cire perdue) technique. The head is a little under life size and is made in a naturalistic style. It has a headdress, suggesting a crown, of complex construction. The main tubular section of the crown runs around the head in a three-layer composition. The upper layer has a band of four horizontal rectangles representing flat, discoidal beads surmounted by a red-painted tubular bead and a tassel. The central layer has a row of vertical rectangles representing tubular beads with tassels. The lower layer has a row of rosettes painted in red. The main tubular section of the crown also has a projecting arc around the forehead comprising small tubular beads edged with a row of red-painted feathers. At the back of the crown is a neck cover. The central part of the cover has eighteen vertical elements incised to indicate plaitwork with traces of black paint. The bottom and sides have a row of red-painted rosettes. A crest rises above the central crown at the front. It comprises a conical roundel with central boss surrounded by seven concentric rings, probably representing beads. A plaitwork element rises behind the roundel, it terminates in a pointed ovoid tip; both elements bear traces of black paint. The face is slightly elongated and has vertical incised markings (striations). Two lines of holes run between the lobes of the ears, one going through the angle of the neck and the jaw, the second crossing the jaw below the lower lip. A double line of holes runs across the upper lip. The almond-shaped eyes are small in proportion to the head and the eyebrow-ridge is sharply defined. The lips are not striated. There are grooves round the front of the neck representing skin creases. There are holes pierced through the neck at the front and at both sides. There is a large irregular hole on the right proper side of the jaw.
persona2_adapted:
**Adapted Text:**

This head is made of brass (a heavily leaded zinc-brass alloy). It was cast using the lost wax (cire perdue) technique. The head is slightly smaller than life size. It is made in a naturalistic style.

The headdress suggests a crown. Its construction is complex. The main tubular section of the crown goes around the head in three layers.

- The upper layer has a band of four horizontal rectangles. These represent flat, discoidal beads. Above them is a red-painted tubular bead and a tassel.
- The central layer has a row of vertical rectangles. These represent tubular beads with tassels.
- The lower layer has a row of rosettes painted in red.

The main tubular section of the crown also has a projecting arc around the forehead. This arc is made of small tubular beads. It is edged with a row of red-painted feathers.

At the back of the crown is a neck cover. The central part of the cover has eighteen vertical elements. These are incised (carved with lines) to indicate plaitwork (braided pattern). There are traces of black paint. The bottom and sides of the cover have a row of red-painted rosettes.

A crest rises above the central crown at the front. It consists of a conical roundel with a central boss (raised center). This is surrounded by seven concentric rings, probably representing beads. Behind the roundel, a plaitwork element rises. It ends in a pointed ovoid (egg-shaped) tip. Both elements have traces of black paint.

The face is slightly elongated. It has vertical incised markings (striations). Two lines of holes run between the lobes of the ears. One line goes through the angle of the neck and the jaw. The second line crosses the jaw below the lower lip. A double line of holes runs across the upper lip.

The almond-shaped eyes are small in proportion to the head. The eyebrow-ridge is sharply defined. The lips are not striated (no incised lines). There are grooves around the front of the neck. These represent skin creases. There are holes pierced through the neck at the front and on both sides. There is a large irregular hole on the right side of the jaw (the viewer's right).

CuratorialText: Goddess. A standing figure of a female deity (Tārā) solid cast in bronze and gilded. The figure has a high tubular coiffure (jatamukuta) held in place by a medallion flanked by makara-s (water creatures). The medallion was probably set with stones, as were the eyes. The earlobes are elongated and the lower body dressed in a tight-fitting cloth knotted at the hips. The upper body is uncovered. The proper right hand is in varadamudrā (gesture of giving), the left hand in katakahastamudrā (the hand pose wherein the tips of the fingers are applied to the thumb so as to form a ring, as if to hold a flower). The two middle fingers of the right hand are missing as are toes from both feet.
persona2_adapted: Goddess. A standing figure of a female deity (Tārā), solid cast in bronze and gilded. The figure has a high tubular coiffure (jatamukuta), held in place by a medallion flanked by makara-s (water creatures). The medallion was probably set with stones, as were the eyes. The earlobes are elongated. The lower body is dressed in a tight-fitting cloth, knotted at the hips. The upper body is uncovered. The proper right hand is in varadamudrā (gesture of giving). The left hand is in katakahastamudrā (the hand pose where the tips of the fingers are applied to the thumb to form a ring, as if to hold a flower). The two middle fingers of the right hand are missing, as are toes from both feet.

CuratorialText:
Fragment of a marble corner palmetto with bird foot.
The small fragment originates from the standing, rectangular snapped off corner palmetto above a volute acroterion. One both sides of the middle leave, which protrudes from the corner, three lightly curved and hollow shaped leaves are visible and whose edges are accompanied by a bar. The round endings were emphasised on the outer side by tubercle. Darts appear between the leaves. On the left side of the fragment remains of a curved ovolo moulding of the volute is preserved. The upper and back side show a small pentagonal block with a 45^ (degrees) outstanding, discs-like tunnel. Leaves with a convex back site seem to grow out of the tunnel. On the block and on both sides of the tunnels two claws are visible with bird legs on each the edges
On the darts on the left side traces of red paint was found on the uppermost and the third. Traces of red and blue paint.
The light crystalline marble is fairly sanded on the surface, however, the underside is fractured.
persona2_adapted:
Fragment of a marble corner palmetto with bird foot.

This small fragment comes from a standing, rectangular corner palmetto that was snapped off. It was originally located above a volute acroterion. On both sides of the central leaf—which protrudes from the corner—three slightly curved, hollow-shaped leaves are visible. Their edges are accompanied by a bar. The rounded ends are emphasized on the outer side by a tubercle. Darts appear between the leaves. On the left side of the fragment, part of a curved ovolo molding from the volute is preserved.

The upper and back sides show a small pentagonal block. This block has a disc-like tunnel that projects outward at a 45-degree angle. Leaves with a convex back side seem to grow out of this tunnel. On the block and on both sides of the tunnel, two claws are visible. Each claw has bird legs on its edges.

On the darts on the left side, traces of red paint were found on the uppermost dart and the third one. Traces of red and blue paint are also present.

The light crystalline marble is fairly sanded on the surface. However, the underside is fractured.

CuratorialText: Chess piece; walrus ivory; king: bearded; wearing floriated crown, tunic, mantle and brooch; seated upon high-backed chair holding sheathed sword; chair back: two horizontal compartments separated by zigzag band; upper filled with interlaced arcading of round arches; lower filled with symmetrical foliate ornaments; chair sides decorated with interlaced patterns.
persona2_adapted: Chess piece; walrus ivory; king: bearded. He wears a floriated crown, tunic, mantle, and brooch. He is seated on a high-backed chair, holding a sheathed sword. The back of the chair has two horizontal compartments, separated by a zigzag band. The upper compartment is filled with interlaced arcading of round arches. The lower compartment is filled with symmetrical foliate ornaments. The sides of the chair are decorated with interlaced patterns.

CuratorialText: Egyptian terracotta model palmette acroterion. The upper corner broken from a miniature altar, decorated on each side with a half-palmette. Brown Nile silt with a light-brown core, abundant fine gold mica and some white inclusions.
persona2_adapted: Egyptian terracotta model palmette acroterion. The upper corner was broken from a miniature altar. Each side is decorated with a half-palmette. CuratorialText: Painting. Oil painting on canvas. The adoration of the Śālagrāma is shown in the middle of the picture. To the left, is Balakṛṣṇa holding the butter ball. Other ritual objects typically used in 'puja' can be seen too: a copper water-pourer and a bell.

CuratorialText: Pottery Protocorinthian alabastron decorated with a lion and two griffins; wheel-made; depressed-ovoid body on a rounded base; small mouth with a sloping rim; pierced lug handle at the top; made of fine buff clay and slip, with painted decoration which has fired black; also added purple paint on the animal bodies; details of the figures are incised. The main frieze shows two griffins separated by stylised tree-like motif; a lion occupies the space between the two mythical beasts; filling motifs in the field. Above the main scene is a frieze of stylised floral designs; below simpler motifs composed of dots separated by S-shaped strokes. Simple rays on the vase, and tongues on the lip.
persona2_adapted:
**Adapted Text:**

This Protocorinthian alabastron is made of pottery. It is wheel-made. The body is depressed-ovoid and sits on a rounded base. The mouth is small with a sloping rim. At the top, there is a pierced lug handle. The vase is made of fine buff clay and slip. The painted decoration has fired black. Purple paint was added on the animal bodies. Details of the figures are incised.

The main frieze shows two griffins. A stylized tree-like motif separates them. A lion occupies the space between the two mythical beasts. There are filling motifs in the field. Above the main scene is a frieze of stylized floral designs. Below are simpler motifs composed of dots separated by S-shaped strokes. Simple rays appear on the vase, and tongues on the lip.

CuratorialText: IWM holds approximately 11 million photographs of modern conflict from the First World War to present day.
persona2_adapted: IWM holds about 11 million photographs. These images document modern conflict from the First World War to the present day.

CuratorialText: The collection covers the activities of British and Commonwealth forces and international perspectives. Photographs from official, press, and private perspectives such as soldiers, nurses and civilians who participated in or witnessed conflict reveal the human impact of war. The collection includes notable photographers such as Olive Edis, Bill Brandt and Tim Hetherington.
persona2_adapted: The collection documents the activities of British and Commonwealth forces, as well as international viewpoints. Photographs come from official, press, and private sources—including soldiers, nurses, and civilians who took part in or observed conflict. These images reveal the human impact of war. The collection features notable photographers such as Olive Edis, Bill Brandt, and Tim Hetherington.

CuratorialText: The photographs can be viewed alongside original documents which help us understand how and why these photographs were taken.

CuratorialText:
Uniforms, Flags, Badges and Insignia
An important and international collection of flags and uniforms, the latter representing all of the armed services and institutions of not only Great Britain and the Commonwealth, but Allies as well as former enemies along with their associated badges and insignia.

Uniforms, Flags, Badges, and Insignia

This is an important and international collection. It includes flags and uniforms. The uniforms represent all armed services and institutions. These come not only from Great Britain and the Commonwealth, but also from Allies and former enemies. The collection also includes their associated badges and insignia.

CuratorialText:
Mementoes and Miscellanea
A diverse collection containing objects of personal significance, including escape and evasion items, prisoner of war and civilian internee items, trench art, battlefield souvenirs, commemorative objects, foodstuffs, currency, tobacciana, toys and games, Kindertransport and Holocaust related items, home-front and peace-time items.
persona2_adapted:
**Mementoes and Miscellanea**

This is a varied collection of objects that hold personal meaning. It includes items related to escape and evasion, objects made by prisoners of war and civilian internees, trench art, souvenirs from battlefields, commemorative pieces, food, currency, tobacco-related items, toys and games, objects connected to the Kindertransport and the Holocaust, as well as items from the home front and peacetime.

CuratorialText:

CuratorialText: IWM’s Sound Collection is home to over 35,000 recordings relating to conflicts since 1914.  It is the largest oral history archive of its kind in the world, with more than 60,000 hours of material and interviews in over 15 different languages.  Beyond interviews it also includes speeches, sound effects, radio broadcasts and music. The collection covers the wartime experiences of ordinary men and women- from soldiers and activists, to factory workers and journalists. It also includes notable figures such as Field Marshal Bernard Montgomery and Vera Lynn.  Our unique collection of oral history interviews has formed the basis for significant academic research as well as featuring heavily in bestselling books, films, TV and radio broadcasts and podcasts. Many thousands of our audio recordings can be listened to via the IWM website, which is regularly updated with new additions.
persona2_adapted:
IWM’s Sound Collection contains over 35,000 recordings related to conflicts since 1914. It is the largest oral history archive of its kind in the world. The collection includes more than 60,000 hours of material and interviews in over 15 languages. In addition to interviews, it also includes speeches, sound effects, radio broadcasts, and music.

The collection documents the wartime experiences of ordinary people—soldiers, activists, factory workers, and journalists. It also includes well-known figures such as Field Marshal Bernard Montgomery and Vera Lynn. Our unique collection of oral history interviews has been the foundation for important academic research. It has also been used extensively in bestselling books, films, television and radio programs, and podcasts.

Many thousands of our audio recordings can be accessed on the IWM website. The website is updated regularly with new additions.

CuratorialText:
IWM has managed a Film Archive for over a century. From its very beginnings as an institution IWM understood the importance of film as primary source material and its extensive collection illustrates the causes, course and consequences of conflict from the First World War to the present day. A remarkable resource for researchers and film-makers
Our constantly growing collection now extends to over 25,000 hours of moving images, representing a wide and diverse range of material from public and service information films, documentaries and unedited combat film, through to official newsreels and amateur films. Two substantial collections from NATO and the United Nations in the former Yugoslavia are also held. An ongoing film digitisation programme provides free online access, with newly-digitised media added as soon as it becomes available.
persona2_adapted:
IWM has managed a Film Archive for over a century. From its earliest days, the institution recognized that film is a primary source material. Its extensive collection shows the causes, course, and consequences of conflict from the First World War to the present day. This is a remarkable resource for researchers and filmmakers.

Our collection continues to grow. It now includes more than 25,000 hours of moving images. This material is wide and diverse: public information films, service information films, documentaries, unedited combat footage, official newsreels, and amateur films. We also hold two large collections from NATO and the United Nations in the former Yugoslavia. An ongoing film digitization program provides free online access. Newly digitized media is added as soon as it becomes available.

CuratorialText:
Private Papers
IWM holds over 20,000 individual collections of private papers, principally comprising unpublished diaries, letters and memoirs written primarily by British and Commonwealth servicemen and women since 1914 and by civilians mainly during the two world wars. We also hold many important documents from victims and survivors of the Holocaust.
persona2_adapted:
Private Papers

IWM holds more than 20,000 individual collections of private papers. These collections mainly consist of unpublished diaries, letters, and memoirs. The authors are primarily British and Commonwealth servicemen and women, writing since 1914, as well as civilians, mainly during the two world wars. We also hold many important documents from victims and survivors of the Holocaust.

CuratorialText:
Foreign and Related Documents
We also look after a large collection of foreign documents, including the official British records of the major war crimes trials conducted at Nuremberg and Tokyo, together with related documents from the Second World War which mainly concern the German and Japanese war efforts.
persona2_adapted:
Foreign and Related Documents

We also maintain a large collection of foreign documents. This includes the official British records of the major war crimes trials held at Nuremberg and Tokyo. We also hold related documents from the Second World War. These documents mainly concern the German and Japanese war efforts.

CuratorialText: This exceptional collection is one of the most important representations of twentieth century British art in the world. It includes many great works of art from the British government war art schemes of the First and Second World Wars, which employed the greatest artists of their day, including leaders of the avant garde. These included Paul Nash, C R W Nevinson, John Singer Sargent and Sir William Orpen.  IWM also holds the unique War Artists Archive, which reveals the day-to-day running of the war art schemes and gives fascinating glimpses of the artists' experiences.
persona2_adapted: This collection is exceptional. It is one of the most important representations of twentieth century British art in the world. It includes many major works from the British government war art schemes of the First and Second World Wars. These schemes employed the greatest artists of their time, including leaders of the avant garde. Among them were Paul Nash, C R W Nevinson, John Singer Sargent, and Sir William Orpen. IWM also holds the unique War Artists Archive. This archive reveals how the war art schemes were run on a daily basis. It also offers fascinating glimpses into the artists' experiences.

CuratorialText: Our Library collection consists of over 150,000 items covering twentieth and twenty-first century conflict involving British and Commonwealth countries. Along with material on military units and campaign histories, we have biographies, manuals, items relating to the economic, social, and cultural aspects of war, journals, newspapers, ephemera, propaganda, and e-resources.
persona2_adapted: Our Library collection contains over 150,000 items. These items cover conflicts from the twentieth and twenty-first centuries that involved British and Commonwealth countries. In addition to materials about military units and campaign histories, we also hold biographies, manuals, and items related to the economic, social, and cultural dimensions of war. The collection includes journals, newspapers, ephemera, propaganda, and electronic resources.

CuratorialText: image: HMS Laurentic is seen sinking in the background, with half of the ship submerged and her bow rising up out of the water. In the foreground is a lifeboat full of sailors illuminated against the dark by a small light. Another lifeboat is visible on the right and small dots of light in the background indicate further lifeboats. In the background on the left, the top of the German U-boat is visible. HMS Laurentic, originally a P&O liner, was equipped with 6" guns in 1939 and converted into an armed merchant cruiser. She was Worsley's first ship and he remained with her until she was sunk off Foreland, Ulster by the U-boat captain Otto Kretschmer (U-99) on the night of 3 November 1940. Worsley painted the original immediatly afterwards, while on survivors leave. The WAAC bought the picture and in August 1942 sent it to South America in a touring exhibition of war art. On the way, however, the ship was torpedoed and the present picture was painted as a replacement, at the request of the WAAC. (From exhibition catalogue, Burstow Gallery, Brighton, 1995)
persona2_adapted:
**Adapted Text:**

The image shows HMS *Laurentic* sinking in the background. Half of the ship is underwater, and its bow rises above the surface. In the foreground, a lifeboat full of sailors is illuminated against the darkness by a small light. Another lifeboat is visible on the right. Small dots of light in the background indicate more lifeboats. On the left side of the background, the top of a German U-boat can be seen.

HMS *Laurentic* was originally a P&O liner. In 1939, it was equipped with 6-inch guns and converted into an armed merchant cruiser. This was Worsley’s first ship. He remained with her until she was sunk off Foreland, Ulster, by U-boat captain Otto Kretschmer (U-99) on the night of 3 November 1940.

Worsley painted the original version immediately afterward, while on survivors’ leave. The WAAC (War Artists' Advisory Committee) bought the painting. In August 1942, they sent it to South America as part of a touring exhibition of war art. However, the ship carrying the painting was torpedoed en route. At the request of the WAAC, the present picture was painted as a replacement.

(Source: Exhibition catalogue, Burstow Gallery, Brighton, 1995)

CuratorialText: image: A view across a tree-lined road to a high wall bordering a railway cutting. The wall has grafitti on it which reads 'Nuclear state = Police state' followed by an anarchy symbol and then 'One World'. Behind the wall to the right is a large industrial building. Further to the right is a junction with a road leading off to the left.'In the summer of 1983 ... I discovered the old train station in Mildmay Grove and started what became a set of ten drawings of the section of line between Canonbury and Kingsland Road. It was while doing the second drawing that I realized the line I was looking at was used several times a week to take nuclear waste from Sizewell [nuclear power station] to Sellafield [nuclear fuel reprocessing plant]. Over the next six weeks, I found it impossible to escape that realization, and it inextricably meshed with every mark I made.' [Eric Rimmington, from the exhibition catalogue 'A World's Waste: Cumbria, Sellafield and Nuclear Reprocessing, A Brewery Arts Centre Touring Exhibition' 1987.] The artist made this series of drawings at a time of intense anti-nuclear protests against both nuclear weapons and the nuclear power industry. The 1979 decision by the British Government to allow American Cruise Missiles to be sited in the UK sparked a series of marches and protests. These included the Greenham Common Women's Peace Camp, started in 1981 and which was at its height in 1983 when the missiles finally arrived at the Berkshire base. The accident at Three Mile Island, a nuclear power station in Pennsylvania, USA, also in 1979, heightened fears about the safety of nuclear power. Nuclear waste and its transport and storage continues to be a focus of protest and anxiety. The Greater London Assembly commissioned a report into the safety of nuclear waste trains in 2001 for this reason.

image: A view across a tree-lined road to a high wall bordering a railway cutting. The wall has graffiti that reads 'Nuclear state = Police state' followed by an anarchy symbol and then 'One World'. Behind the wall to the right is a large industrial building. Further to the right is a junction with a road leading off to the left.

'In the summer of 1983 ... I discovered the old train station in Mildmay Grove and started what became a set of ten drawings of the section of line between Canonbury and Kingsland Road. It was while doing the second drawing that I realized the line I was looking at was used several times a week to take nuclear waste from Sizewell [nuclear power station] to Sellafield [nuclear fuel reprocessing plant]. Over the next six weeks, I found it impossible to escape that realization, and it inextricably meshed with every mark I made.' [Eric Rimmington, from the exhibition catalogue 'A World's Waste: Cumbria, Sellafield and Nuclear Reprocessing, A Brewery Arts Centre Touring Exhibition' 1987.]

The artist made this series of drawings during a period of intense anti-nuclear protests. These protests were against both nuclear weapons and the nuclear power industry. In 1979, the British Government decided to allow American Cruise Missiles to be placed in the UK. This decision caused a series of marches and protests. One example is the Greenham Common Women's Peace Camp, which started in 1981. It was at its peak in 1983, when the missiles finally arrived at the Berkshire base. Also in 1979, the accident at Three Mile Island—a nuclear power station in Pennsylvania, USA—increased fears about the safety of nuclear power. Nuclear waste, and its transport and storage, continues to be a focus of protest and anxiety. For this reason, the Greater London Assembly commissioned a report in 2001 on the safety of nuclear waste trains.

CuratorialText:
Object description
image: Five children are seated around a table in room lit by a large window. An elderly woman sits at the centre with the youngest child on her lap. A large 'Spotted-Dick' pudding is placed on the table with several bowls and cutlery set around it. The woman serves a boy on the far right who holds out a bowl for his portion.
Label
'Mrs Norris, of Cookham, with five of her war family of seven children from London who had by January of 1941 been with her for sixteen months. Mrs Norris is the ideal foster-parent, and there are very many other householders like her. Over a million children have been moved in school parties from the evacuation areas, and with such large numbers, boarding out in billets must always be the principal means of finding homes for them. Given Mrs Norris' friendliness and care, town children readily adapt themselves to their new surroundings.' (title from folder cover, Children in Wartime).
persona2_adapted:
**Object Description**
Five children sit around a table in a room lit by a large window. An elderly woman sits at the center, holding the youngest child on her lap. A large "Spotted-Dick" pudding is placed on the table, with several bowls and cutlery arranged around it. The woman serves a boy on the far right, who holds out a bowl for his portion.

**Label**
"Mrs. Norris, from Cookham, is shown with five of her 'war family'—seven children from London who, by January 1941, had been living with her for sixteen months. Mrs. Norris represents the ideal foster parent, and there are many other householders like her. More than one million children have been moved in school groups from evacuation areas. With such large numbers, placing children in private homes (called 'billets') must remain the main method of finding homes for them. Because of Mrs. Norris's friendliness and care, city children quickly adapt to their new surroundings." (Title from folder cover: *Children in Wartime*).

**Notes on Cultural References**
- **Spotted-Dick pudding**: A traditional British steamed dessert made with suet and dried fruit (often currants or raisins). The name is a colloquial term; the dish itself is a common, homely food.
- **War family**: Refers to children evacuated from cities during World War II to live with host families in safer rural areas.
- **Billets**: A military term used here to mean private homes where evacuees were temporarily housed.

CuratorialText: image: portrait of a female factory worker operating a lathe. Miss Loftus had been brought to the attention of the War Artist's Advisory Committee as 'an outstanding factory worker'. Knight expected to do a studio portrait but the Ministry of Supply requested that she be painted at work in the Royal Ordnance Factory in Newport. Making a Bofors Breech ring was considered the most highly skilled job in the factory, normally requiring eight or nine years training. Loftus was aged 21 at the time of the painting and had no previous factory experience. Her ability to operate the machine presented a considerable publicity coup at the time. However, it has been suggested that she was placed at the machine precisely for this reason. Industrial machinery was a wholly new element in Knight's work but her technical accuracy was praised in contemporary reports: Knight, like Loftus, was proving herself in a traditionally male environment. Loftus was an outstanding factory worker who had mastered complex engineering skills in a very short space of time, and Knight was commissioned to paint her at work in the factory. Knight was fascinated by circus artistes and dancers, and she emphasises the balance and posture of her subject at work. Industrial machinery was a wholly new element in Knight's work but her technical accuracy was praised in contemporary reports: Knight, like Loftus, was proving herself in a traditionally male environment.

A key theme of his work is warfare, influenced by his early experiences of air raids in Plymouth and subsequent National Service. This is one of a series of six prints entitled 'The Driver'.

CuratorialText:
image: The Royal Navy destroyer HMS Eclipse in the process of sinking in the Aegean Sea. The ship appears to have broken in two, with only the stern and bow sections above water. The bow section is on fire, with a bright cloud of flames and smoke billowing up into the night sky. There is another ship in the background and two Greek islands are visible to the left and right.
Label
On 23 October 1943 the two destroyers HMS Eclipse and HMS Petard sailed along the Turkish coast bound for Leros, one of the Greek Dodecanese Islands, which was occupied by a British garrison. On board the Eclipse was Brigadier-General George Davy, the Director of Military Operations, Middle East, who was enroute to inspect the defences of Leros. The ships were also carrying troops, ammunition and supplies for the garrison. At 11.45pm the Eclipse struck a mine between Kos and Kalymnos, blew up and sank in only two minutes. In addition to the ship's complement of around 200 men, she was carrying another 200 soldiers, including a company of the Buffs. Only around 100 men were picked up by an air-sea rescue launch of the RAF dispatched from Leros and boats sent from HMS Petard, after spending over three hours in the water. Davy was amongst the survivors. He went onto become the Commander of Land Forces, Adriatic, retiring from the Army in 1949. He recorded this traumatic event 24 years after it had occurred.
persona2_adapted:
On 23 October 1943, two destroyers—HMS *Eclipse* and HMS *Petard*—sailed along the Turkish coast. Their destination was Leros, one of the Greek Dodecanese Islands. At that time, a British garrison occupied Leros. On board the *Eclipse* was Brigadier-General George Davy, the Director of Military Operations, Middle East. He was traveling to inspect the defences of Leros. The ships also carried troops, ammunition, and supplies for the garrison.

At 11:45 PM, the *Eclipse* struck a mine between Kos and Kalymnos. The ship exploded and sank in only two minutes. In addition to its own crew of about 200 men, the *Eclipse* carried another 200 soldiers, including a company of the Buffs. Only about 100 men survived. They were rescued by an air-sea rescue launch from the RAF, dispatched from Leros, and by boats sent from HMS *Petard*. The survivors had spent over three hours in the water.

Davy was among the survivors. He later became the Commander of Land Forces, Adriatic, and retired from the Army in 1949. He recorded this traumatic event 24 years after it occurred.

CuratorialText: Acknowledged as one of the greatest landscape artists of all time, Joseph Mallord William Turner (1775–1851) knew Oxford extremely well. From childhood, he made numerous drawings and watercolours of the streets and colleges and the picturesque views surrounding the city. His celebrated oil painting of the High Street, Oxford is on display in the 19th-Century Art Gallery. To mark the 250th anniversary of J.M.W. Turner's birth on 23 April 1775, explore this selection of his depictions of Oxford from the Ashmolean's collections. The Museum's Western Art Print Room houses just over 100 watercolours and drawings by Turner, as well as many landscape etchings. There are lively studies of animals, birds and fish, as well as evocative views of places that the artist visited on his travels in Britain and Europe, from Margate to Venice.

CuratorialText: In the second millennium BCE the island of Crete was home to a complex pre-Greek society known as ‘the Minoans’. When archaeologist Sir Arthur Evans started to excavate at the site of Knossos on Crete, in 1900, he frequently connected the things he found with ancient Cretan myths. He named the building at Knossos the ‘Palace of Minos’, after the mythical king of Crete, and connected it to the story of the Labyrinth. The Labyrinth was an ingenious maze commissioned by King Minos to imprison the ferocious Minotaur, a creature with the head of a bull and the body of a man. In the myth, the Minotaur is killed by Theseus, the famous semi-divine hero. Following Theseus's victory, he returned to Greece, and became king and the founder of Athens. The Cretan myths originated in stories which the Greek and Roman inhabitants of the city of Knossos would have told long after the Palace had been destroyed.
persona2_adapted: In the second millennium BCE, the island of Crete was home to a complex pre-Greek society known as 'the Minoans.' When archaeologist Sir Arthur Evans began excavating at Knossos on Crete in 1900, he often connected the objects he found to ancient Cretan myths. He named the building at Knossos the 'Palace of Minos,' after the mythical king of Crete, and linked it to the story of the Labyrinth. The Labyrinth was a cleverly designed maze. King Minos ordered it to be built to imprison the fierce Minotaur, a creature with the head of a bull and the body of a man. In the myth, the Minotaur is killed by Theseus, the famous semi-divine hero. After his victory, Theseus returned to Greece, became king, and founded Athens. The Cretan myths originated in stories that the Greek and Roman inhabitants of Knossos would have told long after the Palace had been destroyed.

CuratorialText:
Landscape painting has a long tradition in East Asian art. However, landscape did not become an important feature of Japanese woodblock prints until the early 1800s. By this time, Japanese citizens were taking part in more outdoor sightseeing activities, such as temple visits and boat trips, and there was a growing demand for souvenir images. In the competitive world of the publishing industry, publishers were constantly trying to develop new products; landscape prints were an appealing novelty.

Technical factors also played an important part in the emergence of the landscape print. Particularly significant was the introduction of the new imported pigment Prussian Blue. Unlike earlier Japanese blues, which faded very quickly, this brilliant colour gave artists more freedom of expression, especially in the depiction of sky and water

Here we feature a selection of highlights from the master of landscape prints, Utagawa Hiroshige, as well as selected works by Hokusai and Kuniyoshi.
persona2_adapted:
Landscape painting has a long tradition in East Asian art. However, landscape did not become an important feature of Japanese woodblock prints until the early 1800s. By this time, Japanese citizens were taking part in more outdoor sightseeing activities, such as temple visits and boat trips. There was a growing demand for souvenir images. In the competitive world of the publishing industry, publishers were constantly trying to develop new products. Landscape prints were an appealing novelty.

Technical factors also played an important role in the emergence of the landscape print. Particularly significant was the introduction of the new imported pigment Prussian Blue. Unlike earlier Japanese blues, which faded very quickly, this brilliant colour gave artists more freedom of expression. This was especially true in the depiction of sky and water.

Here we feature a selection of highlights from the master of landscape prints, Utagawa Hiroshige. We also include selected works by Hokusai and Kuniyoshi.

CuratorialText:
The collection of antiquities includes internationally renowned archaeological collections from Prehistoric Europe, the Ancient Near East, Ancient Egypt and Sudan, Minoan Aegean, Mycenaean and Classical Greece, Ancient Cyprus, the Roman world, Medieval and later Europe, as well as the museum's Founding Collection and historic archives.

It also includes one of the oldest, largest and best-preserved collections of casts of Greek and Roman sculpture in the UK.

In addition, it holds one of the oldest, largest, and best-preserved collections of casts of Greek and Roman sculpture in the UK.

CuratorialText: The Western Art collections contain outstanding European fine and decorative arts from the Renaissance to the present day. They include Old Master and later paintings, drawings, watercolours, and prints; English and continental ceramics, sculpture, silver, watches, rings and musical instruments. The Ashmolean's exceptional print collection contains around 25,000 drawings and over 250,000 prints and many are accessible through our Western Art Print Room.

CuratorialText: The painting resulted from an important early commission for two watercolours to be engraved for the annual calendar published by the University Press, the Oxford Almanack. They were so successful that Tuner eventually completed ten watercolours between 1798 and 1804. This work and one other were later painted in oils for the local print-seller and frame-maker, James Wyatt. Turner consulted Wyatt on the details of the architecture included in the view. He also introduced figures, members of the University and clergy, and some women ‘for the sake of colour’. The painting is one of the most fully documented of all Turner’s works because Wyatt kept his correspondence with the artist which was included in his posthumous sale. The original purpose of the commission was to have the design engraved. Wyatt settled on an oil painting, instead of a watercolour, half the size of Turner’s normal canvases, at a cost of 100 guineas. The painting was exhibited at the Royal Academy in 1812. When, in the 1830s, Turner was choosing views of Oxford for his greatest series of watercolours, he rejected the High Street. He felt that in this painting he had achieved an unparalleled view of technical mastery that he could not repeat.
